Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

O Prophet, fear Allah and do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites. Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise. (1)

The addressing of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) here as 'O Prophet' (Ya ayyuhan-Nabi) is not merely for honor and respect, as people generally assume, but is used here to remind him of his official duty. The meaning is: you are the Prophet and Messenger of Allah; therefore, you should only care about your Lord. Fear only Allah, and convey His message to the people, completely indifferent to the opposition of the disbelievers and the hypocrites. A similar address has passed in Surah al-Ma'idah: 'O Messenger, convey that which has been revealed to you from your Lord' (al-Ma'idah: 67).

'And do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites.' This warning and emphasis were not given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) because of any apprehension that he might be influenced or intimidated by the disbelievers and hypocrites, God forbid. Rather, by addressing the Prophet, a warning is given to the opponents that no matter how much force these wicked people use and whatever trials they stir up, you must not pay any attention to their words. It should be noted that the upcoming verses mention certain issues that the disbelievers and hypocrites used as a means of spreading mischief against you.

Here, mentioning the disbelievers and the hypocrites together highlights the reality that both are fundamentally of the same nature. Both are united in their hostility toward Islam. The only difference is that one opposes openly, while the other claims to be a Muslim, enters among the Muslims, and tries to uproot Islam from within. Therefore, in terms of their ultimate end, there is no difference between the two; in fact, the Qur'an states that the hypocrites will be in the lowest depths of the Fire.

'Indeed, Allah is ever Knowing and Wise.' This is the proof: why should the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), completely fearless and indifferent to all others, follow and execute only what Allah has commanded? It states that Allah is All-Knowing and All-Wise. Therefore, whatever He has commanded is based on true knowledge and wisdom. The vanities and nonsense that people utter against it are unworthy of attention. Further explanation of this will follow in verses 39 and 48.

And follow what is revealed to you from your Lord. Indeed, Allah is ever, of what you do, Aware. And rely upon Allah; and sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs. (2-3)

What was stated in the previous verse in a negative form is expressed here in a positive manner: completely indifferent to all the mischief-making of the wicked, follow the revelation that comes to you from your Lord, and rest assured that Allah is fully aware of your every step and action. In this verse, the first address is singular ('follow'), and the second is plural ('of what you do'). This points to the reality that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is addressed here as the representative of the community, showing that Muslims must also adopt this exact approach in matters of religion.

'And rely upon Allah; and sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.' Meaning, since every word and decree of Allah is based on wisdom and He is fully aware of everything, place your trust in Him alone and stand firm on your stance. Allah is sufficient for reliance and trust; in His presence, you require no other support. We have explained the word 'Wakil' (Disposer of affairs) elsewhere: it refers to the Entity upon whom one places absolute trust and to whom one surrenders all affairs. Allah's demand of His servants is that they execute His commands under all circumstances and rely on Him, trusting that He will enable them to overcome any difficulties they face in this path.

Allah has not made for a man two hearts in his interior. And He has not made your wives whom you declare unlawful (by zihar) your mothers. And He has not made your adopted sons your [true] sons. That is [merely] your saying by your mouths; but Allah says the truth, and He guides to the [right] way. (4)

Following the introduction, this points to certain issues that the disbelievers and hypocrites used as a means of spreading mischief against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).

'Allah has not made for a man two hearts in his interior.' This serves as an introduction to the subsequent points. Since these points display a contradiction of thought and will, this is introduced to show the absurdity of such a contradiction: had Allah intended for man to remain trapped in the conflict of contradictory wills, He would have given him more than one heart, but He did not make two hearts in any person's interior. This clearly means He did not approve of man harboring completely contradictory wills within himself. Yet, contrary to this natural structure, human beings in their kuji-fahm (distorted reasoning) harbor contradictory wills: claiming faith in God while worshipping others, or pledging loyalty to the Messenger while conspiring with his enemies. If a person has only one heart, his actions must be consistent; any contradiction is proof of a spiritual ailment that must be corrected.

'And He has not made your wives whom you declare unlawful (by zihar) your mothers.' As an example of this contradiction, the practice of zihar is mentioned: if a person declares zihar to his wife, she does not become his mother, yet the people of jahiliyyah treated such wives as prohibited like mothers. When the Qur'an corrected this, the disbelievers and hypocrites tried to stir up mischief against the Prophet, accusing him of permitting what was historically prohibited.

'Zihar' was an ancient Arab practice where a husband would say to his wife, 'You are to me like the back of my mother' (anti 'alayya ka-zahri ummee), which made her permanently prohibited to him. The word is derived from 'zahr' (back). If a husband associated any part of his wife's body with his prohibited female relatives, the same rule applied. In jahiliyyah, this was treated as an absolute, irrevocable divorce. The Qur'an corrected this (as detailed in Surah al-Mujadilah, verses 2-4), declaring it a false and evil statement, and allowed the husband to return to his wife on the condition of expiation (freeing a slave, fasting for two consecutive months, or feeding sixty needy people). This reform saved family structures from collapsing due to rash words, but the opponents used it to accuse the Prophet of erasing the boundary between mothers and wives. The text points to this, showing that only those with a distorted intellect could equate such a rash statement with actual motherhood. An expiation is required as a penalty and a lesson, but destroying the family structure is not justified. Further detailed discussion on the evils of zihar will follow under Surah al-Mujadilah.

'And He has not made your adopted sons your [true] sons.' In their distorted reasoning, the people of jahiliyyah also equated adopted sons with biological sons, prohibiting a man from marrying the divorced or widowed wife of his adopted son. This opposed the natural family structure established by Islam on the principle that biological relations are closer to one another. To dismantle this false custom, Allah commanded the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to marry the divorced wife of his adopted son, Zayd (peace be upon him). Zayd was commonly regarded as his adopted son, so the most effective way to eliminate this custom was for the Prophet himself to take this step. The opponents tried to use this to accuse the Prophet of marrying his son's wife. This is discussed in detail in section 6 ahead.

'That is [merely] your saying by your mouths; but Allah says the truth, and He guides to the [right] way' means these customs are mere empty words of your own fabrication, carrying no basis in intellect, nature, or divine law. Allah is presenting the truth to you through His Prophet, guiding you to the natural straight path, so follow this path and abandon the customs of jahiliyyah.

Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allah. But if you do not know their fathers - then they are your brothers in religion and your patrons. And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful. (5)

'Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allah' (Ud'oohum li-aba'ihim huwa aqsatu 'indallah). Adopted sons must be associated with their actual biological fathers to preserve their lineage, which is more just and upright in the law of Allah. If they are treated as biological sons, the entire system of inheritance, relations, and family structures—which Allah has established on the basis of biological relations and natural human feelings—will be disrupted. The laws of Islam are built on justice and equity; therefore, no custom contrary to this can be tolerated.

'But if you do not know their fathers - then they are your brothers in religion and your patrons' (Fa-illam ta'lamoo aba'ahum fa-ikhwanukum fid-deeni wa mawaleekum). If their biological fathers are unknown, they will be treated as brothers in faith and as patrons (mawali). Islam introduced the concept of spiritual brotherhood, which the Arabs of jahiliyyah did not know. However, the custom of 'wala' (patronage/alliance) existed among them, where a person from outside a tribe could be associated with a family as a 'muly' (patron), gaining mutual rights and responsibilities. The principle 'the patron of a people belongs to them' was accepted, and Islam preserved this custom. The patronage of a freed slave also belonged to the freeing family, who would inherit from him if he had no other heirs.

This shows that if an adopted son's father is unknown, he will be treated as a brother in faith and a patron, but never as a biological son.

'And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful' means any mistake committed in the past out of ignorance is forgiven, as Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful. However, persisting in this practice after this clear warning will be treated as a deliberate sin, for it would be a result of your deliberate intent, for which Allah will surely call you to account.

The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves, and his wives are their mothers. And those of [blood] relationship are closer to one another in the decree of Allah than the [other] believers and the Emigrants, except that you may do to your close associates a kindness. That is in the Book written. (6)

This clarifies the hierarchy of relations that must be maintained in the Islamic society, protecting Muslims from the confusion discussed in the previous verses.

'The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves' (An-Nabiyyu awla bil-mu'mineena min anfusihim). 'Awla' means having a greater right. For example: 'Indeed, the most worthy of Abraham among the people are those who followed him' (Al 'Imran: 68). It means that the Prophet's right over every Muslim is greater than his right over his own self. This is further elaborated in verse 36 of this Surah:

'It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should have any choice about their affair. And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into clear error.' (al-Ahzab: 36)

This shows that once a decree is issued by Allah and His Messenger, no choice remains for any believer. The Messenger speaks as the representative of Allah; therefore, the absolute requirement of faith is to execute his commands without any hesitation, regardless of personal interests, safety, or wealth.

'And his wives are their mothers' (wa azwajuhu ummahatuhum). This is the natural result of the unique relationship between the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his followers. If faith is free from hypocrisy, every Muslim naturally harbors the same feelings of respect, honor, and sanctity for the pure wives of the Prophet as a noble son harbors for his mother. This high status meant that no Muslim could ever conceive of marrying them. Only the hypocrites, for their corrupt ends, entertained such thoughts (discussed ahead). This verse foiled their plans, and is followed in verse 53 by an explicit prohibition: 'And it is not [permissible] for you to harm the Messenger of Allah or to marry his wives after him, ever' (verse 53). We will discuss the spiritual and social wisdom behind this prohibition ahead.

'And those of [blood] relationship are closer to one another in the decree of Allah than the [other] believers and the Emigrants.' After defining the unique status of the Prophet and his wives, the text establishes the biological relation as the basis for inheritance and family rights, as detailed in Surah an-Nisa. 'In the Book of Allah' refers to the verses of inheritance in Surah an-Nisa (verses 7-13, 176).

'Than the [other] believers and the Emigrants' means biological relatives have a greater right over inheritance than other believers and Emigrants, thus ending the temporary arrangement of inheritance based on brotherhood (mu'akhah) established between the Emigrants and the Helpers upon arrival in Madinah.

'Except that you may do to your close associates a kindness. That is in the Book written' allows the believer to leave a portion of his wealth to non-heir relatives or associates as a bequest within the limits established by the law (detailed in Surah an-Nisa).

And [remember, O Muhammad], when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus, the son of Mary; and We took from them a solemn covenant. (7)

The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was commanded in verses 1-2 to follow His revelation and convey it fearlessly. At the end of the section, this is reinforced by referencing the history of past prophets: We took a solemn covenant from all prophets that they would follow the revelation and convey it honestly to their people. This covenant was taken from you, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. By referencing these illustrious messengers, the entire history of prophethood is presented, showing that every prophet and his companions had to carry this heavy burden. The Prophet is mentioned first because he is the primary addressee.

This covenant is detailed throughout the Qur'an, particularly in Surah al-Ma'idah. We cite as an example the covenant taken from Moses (peace be upon him): 'So take them with determination and order your people to take the best of it' (al-A'raf: 145).

'And We took from them a solemn covenant' (Wa akhadhna minhum meethaqan ghaliza). We did not tolerate any compromise or laxity in this matter; rather, We took a firm and heavy covenant from each of them to stand by this duty with determination. Both 'meethaq' (covenant) and 'ghaleez' (solemn/heavy) are used to emphasize its absolute nature.

That He may question the truthful about their truth. And He has prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment. (8)

This defines the wisdom behind taking the covenant: the messengers' honest propagation established the ultimate proof (itmaam al-hujjah), making people deserve that He call the truthful to account for their truth, and punish the disbelievers and hypocrites for their disbelief. Without establishing this proof, punishing people would be contrary to His justice. This is further explained in verses 24 and 37-40 of this Surah, and is identical to: 'Mankind will have no argument against Allah after the messengers' (an-Nisa: 165).

2. The upcoming subject... Verses 9-27

Next, the events of the Battle of the Trench (al-Ahzab) are related to support the central theme: the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his Companions should rely on Allah and not care about their opponents, for He is sufficient. They witnessed His support during this battle: when the allied forces surrounded them and the hypocrites tried to dismantle their unity from within, Allah's plan prevailed, and the enemies were forced to retreat without achieving anything.

The Battle of the Trench occurred in Shawwal, 5 AH. The Jewish leaders of Banu al-Nadir, whom the Prophet had expelled from Madinah to Khaybar, instigated the Quraysh to attack Madinah. When the Quraysh agreed, they also aligned with the tribes of Ghatafan and Hawazin, establishing a joint force of ten thousand soldiers under the command of Abu Sufyan. Furthermore, they convinced the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayzah, who had a peace treaty with the Prophet, to join their alliance. Banu Qurayzah had about eight hundred fighters.

Upon learning of their plans, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), on the advice of Salman al-Farsi (peace be upon him), commanded the Muslims to dig a trench along the vulnerable northern and western sides of Madinah. This three-and-a-half-mile trench was dug with great energy by the three thousand Muslim fighters, and the Prophet himself participated in the labor.

The allied forces besieged Madinah for about a month, but no major battle occurred. Realizing the strength of the Muslim defenses, suffering from internal division, and struck by a violent storm that uprooted their tents, the allied forces lost heart and Abu Sufyan ordered a retreat. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allah upon you when there came against you hosts, then We sent upon them a wind and hosts you did not see. And ever is Allah, of what you do, Seeing. (9)

To reinforce 'And rely upon Allah' (verse 3), the text points to His recent support during the Battle of the Trench, when all Arab tribes rose against the Muslims, yet they were spared. Allah sent a wind and His invisible hosts to disperse the enemy, showing that He is sufficient. This is the proof: if His support is with you, no opposition can harm you.

'Then We sent upon them a wind' (Fa-arsalna 'alayhim reeha). Historical accounts record that during the final days of the siege, a violent storm struck, uprooting their tents, dispersing their animals, and making it impossible to light fires in the cold. أبو سفيان realized that their only option was to save themselves and order a retreat.

'And hosts you did not see' (wa junoodal-lam tarawha) refers to His angels who accompany the believers. Although they are invisible, their support is manifest, reassuring the believers and striking terror into the hearts of their enemies. We have discussed this under Surah al-Anfal (verse 9). While the Muslims did not see them, it is highly likely that the disbelievers saw them, which struck them with terror and made them think the Muslim forces were massive.

'And ever is Allah, of what you do, Seeing' (Wa kanallahu bi-ma ta'maloona Baseera) is identical to verse 2: 'Indeed, Allah is ever, of what you do, Aware' (Innahu kanallahu bi-ma ta'maloona Khabeera). A servant can place his complete trust in Allah only when he has certain faith that He is All-Knowing and All-Seeing, observing the struggles of His servants.

[Remember] when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted [in fear], and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions. (10)

'When they came at you from above you and from below you' refers to the eastern high ground and the western lower ground of Madinah. The allied forces attacked from both directions: the tribe of Ghatafan from the east, and the Quraysh and their allies from the west.

'And when eyes shifted, and hearts reached the throats' is a depiction of extreme fear and panic. In Arabic, 'za'ghal-basar' is used to describe eyes shifting in terror, unable to focus. Similarly, 'hearts reaching the throats' (balaghatil-quloobul-hanajir) depicts extreme distress and anxiety.

'And you assumed about Allah assumptions' (wa tazunnoona billahiz-zunona), although general in wording, refers to those who were weak in faith or were hypocrites. Witnessing the massive army, they panicked and lost hope, doubting His promises and support. The context ahead shows that this refers to the weak-minded and the hypocrites. Regarding the true believers, the Qur'an praises their steadfastness ahead in verse 22: 'And when the believers saw the allied forces, they said, "This is what Allah and His Messenger had promised us..."' Since the majority of the Muslims were of this character, the reference here is specifically to those with weak faith. Therefore, we reject those narratives that suggest the entire Muslim community lost hope.

There the believers were tried and shaken with a severe shaking. (11)

Meaning, these conditions subjected the believers to a severe trial, shaking them deeply. They faced the enemy's yuryish (onslaught) on one hand, and the presence of weak-minded individuals who doubted His promises on the other. But such tests are necessary to distinguish between the sincere and the false claimants. This test is His established law.

And [remember] when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease said, "Allah and His Messenger did not promise us except delusion," (12)

This details 'you assumed about Allah assumptions' (verse 10): the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts began to spread doubt among the Muslims, saying: 'Allah and His Messenger promised us the treasures of Caesar and Chosroes, yet we cannot even go out of our homes safely.' This shows how they tried to weaken the Muslims' resolve. This was indeed a severe trial.

There is a subtle point here: 'the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease' (al-munafiqoona walladheena fee quloobihim-marad). In the Qur'an, 'disease' (marad) is used for both hypocrisy and moral malice/hostility. When coupled with 'hypocrites' (as here), it refers to moral malice. The hypocrites included two groups: those who were weak in determination, and those who harbored deep malice and hostility toward Islam, acting as secret agents of the enemy. Recognizing this distinction is necessary to understand the force of various verses.

And when a faction of them said, "O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you, so return." And a group of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, "Indeed, our houses are vulnerable," while they were not vulnerable. They did not intend except to flee. (13)

This refers to another group among the hypocrites—the Bedouins around Madinah, described in Surah at-Tawbah as 'most severe in disbelief and hypocrisy.' They claimed to believe to protect their interests, but would avoid any battle or try to undermine the Muslims' resolve. Some of them came to the Trench to maintain appearances, but spent their energy on weakening the Muslims.

'O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you, so return' (Ya ahla Yathriba la muqama lakum farji'oo). Their addressing the Muslims as 'people of Yathrib' shows they were Bedouins from outside Madinah. Had they been residents, they would have addressed them differently. 'Yathrib' was the ancient name of Madinah, replaced by 'Madinatun-Nabi' (the City of the Prophet) or 'Madinah' after migration. The Bedouins continued to use the ancient name, hoping the Muslim control would be temporary.

'There is no stability for you, so return' means they tried to discourage the Muslims, saying: 'You cannot face this massive army, so abandon your defenses and return to your homes.'

'And a group of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, "Indeed, our houses are vulnerable," while they were not vulnerable. They did not intend except to flee' refers to those Bedouins who sought permission to leave, claiming their homes in the outskirts were vulnerable. This is also proof that they were Bedouins, as the residents of Madinah had already secured their women and children, as recorded in history. The text states: 'Their excuse was false; their homes were not vulnerable, but they simply wanted to flee from the battle.'

And if entry had been made upon them from its sides and then they had been demanded to commit fitnah, they would have done it, and they would not have hesitated about it except a little. And they had already covenanted with Allah before [that] they would not turn their backs. And ever is the covenant of Allah questioned. (14-15)

The pronoun 'its sides' (aqtariha) refers to their 'houses' (buyoot), as is clear from the context. It means: 'Their concern for their homes is a mere excuse; their real concern is to avoid defending the faith. If an enemy force surrounds their homes and demands that they commit fitnah (rebellion or apostasy), they will immediately comply without hesitation.' 'Fitnah' here refers to apostasy or allying with the enemy against the Muslims, as is also explained in Surah an-Nisa (verse 91). This reveals their true nature.

'And they had already covenanted with Allah before [that] they would not turn their backs. And ever is the covenant of Allah questioned' (Wa laqad kanoo 'ahadulla-ha min qablu la yuwalloonal-adbar; wa kana 'ahdulla-hi mas'oola). While embracing Islam implies a covenant to sacrifice everything in His cause, these hypocrites had made a specific covenant. Previously, they had avoided participating in battles using various excuses, but had sworn solemn oaths that they would stand firm in future battles. The text rebukes them: 'You made solemn promises to stand firm, so why do you seek excuses to flee now? You must remember that every covenant with Allah will be questioned.'

Say, "Never will fleeing benefit you if you should flee from death or killing; and then you would not be given enjoyment except a little." Say, "Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He intends for you ill or intends for you mercy?" And they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or any helper. (16-17)

Linguistically, a matching phrase is omitted before 'or intends for you mercy,' which has been clarified in the translation. The meaning is: if you try to escape from His decree, who can protect you if He intends to seize you, or who can block His mercy if He intends to bless you? Mercy and punishment are entirely in His hands; therefore, fleeing from Him is pointless.

'And they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or any helper' is a severe warning: if He seizes them, neither their false protectors (such as idols) nor their tribal alliances can save them.

Already Allah knows the hinderers among you and those who say to their brothers, "Come to us," and they do not join the battle except a little, being stingy toward you. (18-19)

'Already Allah knows' (Qad ya'lamullahu) is prefixed with emphasis. It is a severe warning: Allah has always been fully aware of those who discourage others from the battle, inviting them to stay away. They do not participate in the defense except for appearances, and when they do, they are extremely stingy toward you.

This refers to those hypocrites who not only avoided the battle themselves but also discouraged their close associates, telling them to stay with them to avoid danger. Since the trench line was long, they found many opportunities to avoid their duties and encourage others to do the same.

'And they do not join the battle except a little' means they participated only for appearances, while actively discouraging others.

Being stingy toward you. Then when fear comes, you see them looking at you, their eyes rolling like one who is fainting from death. But when fear departs, they lash you with sharp tongues, stingy toward [any] good. Those have not believed, so Allah has rendered their deeds worthless, and that for Allah is easy. (19)

'Being stingy toward you' (ashihhatan 'alaykum) is conjoined to 'they do not join the battle except a little.' They had no intention of making any sacrifice; they wanted to be counted among the fighters without risking anything.

'Then when fear comes, you see them looking at you, their eyes rolling like one who is fainting from death' means the moment a danger appeared on the front, they panicked in such a manner that their eyes rolled as if they were facing death. True courage belongs only to those who stand firm for His cause, while the pretense of the hypocrites is exposed at the slightest danger.

'But when fear departs, they lash you with sharp tongues, stingy toward [any] good' (Fa-idha dhahabal-khawfu salaqukum bi-alsinatin hidadin ashihhatan 'alal-khayr). 'Salaq' means to speak with sharp, biting language. When the danger passed, they would speak with great arrogance and tundu (sharpness), as if they were the heroes of the battle.

'Stingy toward [any] good' (ashihhatan 'alal-khayr) means they showed this tundu and arrogance solely to demand a greater share in the spoils of war. This is identical to 'stingy toward you' in the previous sentence: they show no sacrifice in danger, but are highly greedy when it is time to divide the spoils, as has been mentioned in other Surahs.

'Those have not believed, so Allah has rendered their deeds worthless, and that for Allah is easy' means since their claim of faith was mere pretense, all the deeds they performed for appearances are rendered worthless, and they will receive no reward for them in the hereafter. Allah accepts only those deeds performed for His pleasure.

'And that for Allah is easy' (Wa kana dhalika 'alallahi yaseera) refutes a common misconception: people often value outward actions and assume that a grand action must be rewarded, but Allah evaluates the intent. He has no need for anyone's deeds, and rejecting insincere deeds is simple for Him. Some assume His mercy means He will accept whatever is presented, but His glory is free from such compromise. This has been explained in Surah al-Baqarah.

They think the allied forces have not departed. And if the allied forces should come [again], they would wish they were in the desert among the Bedouins, inquiring [from there] about your news. And if they should be among you, they would not fight except a little. (20)

This further details their banzili (cowardice): although the allied forces have retreated to their respective lands, the fear of those forces is so deeply entrenched in the hearts of the hypocrites that they think they are still surrounding Madinah.

'And if the allied forces should come [again], they would wish they were in the desert among the Bedouins, inquiring [from there] about your news' means if another attack occurs, they would prefer to flee to the desert and live among the Bedouins, asking from there how you are faring in Madinah.

'And if they should be among you, they would not fight except a little' is comfort for the Muslims: do not worry if they leave you, for even if they remain among you, they are of no benefit in battle. Review the commentary on verse 18.

There has already been for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day and [who] remembers Allah much. (21)

This is a rebuke to those cowards: the Messenger of Allah was present with you on the front, and you witnessed the determination and courage with which he faced all dangers; why then did you not follow his excellent example, but chose to remain so terrified!

'For anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day and [who] remembers Allah much' (li-man kana yarjullaha wal-Yawmal-Akhira wa dhakarallaha katheera). This points to the reality that following the Prophet's example requires certain faith in Allah and the afterlife, and keeping this faith alive through constant remembrance. This shows that the hypocrites lacked this faith, which is why their fear persisted even after the enemy's retreat.

And when the believers saw the allied forces, they said, "This is what Allah and His Messenger had promised us, and Allah and His Messenger spoke the truth." And it did not increase them except in faith and submission. (22)

While verse 12 quoted the hypocrites' doubt that Allah and His Messenger promised them nothing but delusion, this verse describes the response of the true believers: when they saw the allied forces, they reassured themselves that this was the very trial Allah and His Messenger had promised them—referring to the verses stating that success requires passing through severe trials. For example, in Surah al-Baqarah:

'Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you? They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, "When is the victory of Allah?" Unquestionably, the victory of Allah is near.' (al-Baqarah: 214)

In Surah al-Ankabut:

'Do the people think that they will be left to say, "We believe" and they will not be tried? And We have already tried those before them, and Allah will surely make evident those who are truthful, and He will surely make evident the liars.' (al-Ankabut: 2-3)

'And Allah and His Messenger spoke the truth' means His promise of victory is true, and those who stand firm in this trial will witness His victory.

'And it did not increase them except in faith and submission' means this trial strengthened their faith and devotion, in accordance with the divine law of trials.

Among the believers are men who have been true to their covenant with Allah. Of them is he who has fulfilled his vow [by death], and of them is he who awaits [it]. And they did not alter [the covenant] by any alteration. (23)

We explained under Surah al-A'raf that 'men' (rijal) is used here to denote honor and dignity, referring to 'men of truth.'

'Nuhb' (vow) is used in a wide sense, including vow, covenant, and dedication. Imam Bukhari interpreted it as 'covenant,' which is highly appropriate.

While verse 15 described the hypocrites who broke their covenant to stand firm, this describes the loyal believers who fulfilled their covenant by sacrificing their lives, and those who stand ready to do so, without making any alteration in their covenant.

That Allah may reward the truthful for their truth and punish the hypocrites if He wills or accept their repentance. Indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful. (24)

A verb is omitted before 'That,' meaning: 'Allah sent this trial to distinguish between the truthful and the hypocrites, so that He may reward the truthful for their truth and punish the hypocrites if He wills, or accept their repentance if they repent.'

'If He wills or accept their repentance' (in sha'a aw yatooba 'alayhim) is conjoined to 'if He wills' according to standard Arabic usage. Allah's will is based on His wisdom; if they repent, He will accept their repentance, for He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful. This invites the hypocrites to repent while there is still time.

And Allah repelled those who disbelieved, in their rage, not having obtained any good. And sufficient was Allah for the believers in battle. And ever is Allah Powerful and Exalted in Might. (25)

'Those who disbelieved' refers to the allied forces. It states that they retreated in their rage without achieving any of their plans to destroy the Muslims. 'Not having obtained any good' (lam yanaloo khayra) means none of their objectives succeeded.

'And sufficient was Allah for the believers in battle' (Wa kafallahul-mu'mineenal-qital) means the Muslims did not have to fight a major battle; Allah Himself sufficed for them by sending a wind and His invisible hosts to disperse the enemy.

'And ever is Allah Powerful and Exalted in Might' (Wa kanallahu Qawiyyan 'Azeeza) is a reminder of 'And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs' (verse 3): He is Powerful and dominant, so His servants should place their complete trust in Him.

And He brought down those who backed them among the People of the Book from their fortresses and cast terror into their hearts [so that] a party you killed and you took captive a party. And He caused you to inherit their land and their homes and their properties and a land which you have not [yet] trodden. And ever is Allah, over all things, Competent. (26-27)

This refers to the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayzah, who had a peace treaty with the Prophet but broke it during the Battle of the Trench to support the allied forces, as instigated by Huyayy ibn Akhtab. The Prophet besieged them for twenty-five days, and they finally submitted to the judgment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, who decided that their active fighters be executed and the rest be taken captive. This was executed.

'From their fortresses' (min sayaseehim). 'Sayasī' literally means the spur of a rooster, and is used for horns, and subsequently for defensive fortresses and castles.

'And cast terror into their hearts' (Wa qadhafa fee quloobihimur-ru'b) means He filled their hearts with such terror that they could not resist Sa'd's judgment, and the Muslims took possession of their lands, homes, and properties.

'And a land which you have not [yet] trodden' (wa ardal-lam tata'ooha) is a prophecy of future conquests—such as Khaybar, Makkah, Syria, and Rome. 'And ever is Allah, over all things, Competent' (Wa kanallahu 'ala kulli shay'in Qadeera) is a proof that He is competent over all things, so place your complete trust in Him.

4. The upcoming subject... Verses 28-35

After addressing the believers, the text addresses the wives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon them), warning them against the mischief-making of the hypocrites, who tried to cultivate worldly desires in their hearts. Since Allah chose them to be the wives of His Prophet, their primary duty is to convey the Book and wisdom revealed in their homes. Their status is high if they fulfill their duties, and their responsibility is equally heavy if they commit any lapse. Read the verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

O Prophet, say to your wives, "If you should desire the worldly life and its adornment, then come, I will provide for you and give you a gracious release. But if you should desire Allah and His Messenger and the home of the Hereafter - then indeed, Allah has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward." (28-29)

Most commentators suggest that after the Conquest of Khaybar, when Muslims achieved prosperity, the Prophet's wives demanded more provision, and these verses were revealed in response. In our view, this is incorrect for several reasons:

First, these verses concern the events of 5 AH—the Battle of the Trench and Banu Qurayzah—and the marriage with Zaynab, all occurring in 5 AH, whereas Khaybar was conquered later.

Second, a simple demand for more provision does not justify an ultimatum of divorce and release.

Third, we cannot assume that the pure wives of the Prophet were possessed by worldly desires to the extent that He had to intervene with such an ultimatum.

In our view, this was a response to the mischief-making of the hypocrites. The female hypocrites would visit the wives of the Prophet, trying to cultivate worldly desires in their hearts and suggesting that if they obtained a divorce, wealthy chiefs of Arabia would marry them. The male hypocrites also tried to spread doubts when Zayd divorced Zaynab and the Prophet married her. The text addresses this: 'Tell your wives that if they desire worldly comfort, I will release them with a gracious release, but if they desire Allah, His Messenger, and the hereafter, He has prepared for them a great reward.'

The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) presented this choice to them, beginning with Aisha, who immediately chose Allah and His Messenger, as did all other wives. This foiled the plans of the hypocrites, proving that the wives of the Prophet chose only Allah, His Messenger, and the hereafter, and could not be swayed by worldly desires. Thus, these verses are not a rebuke of their worldliness, but are a demonstration of their high character.

'But if you should desire Allah and His Messenger and the home of the Hereafter - then indeed, Allah has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward' (Wa in kuntunna turidnallaha wa Rasoolahu wad-Daral-Akhirata fa-innallaha a'adda lil-muhsinati minkunna ajran 'azeema). 'Ahsan' (good/upright conduct) here means fulfilling their duties toward Allah and His Messenger with complete sincerity. This shows that their relation to the Prophet was beneficial only when accompanied by sincere devotion and righteous deeds.

O wives of the Prophet, whoever of you should commit a clear immorality - for her the punishment would be doubled two fold, and that for Allah is easy. (30)

After the Prophet addressed them, Allah addressed them directly: 'O wives of the Prophet, because of your high status, your responsibility is equally heavy. If any of you commits a clear immorality, her punishment will be doubled.'

'And that for Allah is easy' (Wa kana dhalika 'alallahi yaseera) means being the wives of His Messenger will not save anyone from His justice; His law is absolute and without partiality.

This warning does not suggest that they were prone to immorality, God forbid; rather, it was to remind them of their high status and warn them to remain vigilant against the conspiracies of the hypocrites. 'Clear immorality' (fahishatin-mubayyinatin) is used because the hypocrites were trying to create a scandal to damage the moral reputation of the Prophet's household.

And whoever of you devoutly obeys Allah and His Messenger and does righteousness - We will give her her reward twice, and We have prepared for her a noble provision. (31)

'Just as your punishment is doubled for a lapse, your reward is doubled for righteous deeds. Those among you who remain devoted to Allah and His Messenger and do righteous deeds will receive a double reward, and We have prepared for them a noble provision.' 'Noble provision' (rizqan kareema) means it will be granted as their right, eternally and without any accountability.

This shows that accountability is in proportion to the opportunities for guidance received. Since the wives of the Prophet enjoyed his close company, their responsibility was heavy, and their reward for steadfastness was equally great.

O wives of the Prophet, you are not like anyone among women. If you fear [Allah], then do not be soft in speech [to men], lest he in whose heart is disease should covet, but speak with appropriate speech. (32)

'Khudo' ' means to speak with softness and humility. 'Do not be soft in speech' (fala takhda'na bil-qawl) means do not speak with a soft, accommodating tone. While soft speech is generally virtuous, the situation required a different approach. Since the hypocrites were trying to establish a connection with the wives of the Prophet to spread mischief, and would approach them under the guise of sympathy, the wives are instructed: 'You are not like ordinary women; your conduct is a model for others, so do not speak with a soft tone that might encourage those with diseased hearts; rather, speak with a firm, business-like tone.'

'You are not like anyone among women, if you fear [Allah]' (Lastunna ka-ahadim-minan-nisa'i inittaqaytun) means this high status is conditional on their piety; if they maintain piety, they will retain this status.

'Lest he in whose heart is disease should covet' refers to those hypocrites who harbored malice against the Prophet and sought to damage his household's reputation.

'But speak with appropriate speech' (wa qulna qawlam-ma'roofa) means speak in a direct, simple manner without any soft, accommodating tone, showing them that their plans have no room.

And abide in your houses and do not display yourselves as [was] the display of the former times of ignorance. And establish prayer and give zakat and obey Allah and His Messenger. Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification. (33)

These guidelines were given to direct the wives of the Prophet to their high purpose of life, protecting them from the advice of the female hypocrites who tried to cultivate worldly desires in them, encouraging them to display their adornment. The text commands: 'Abide in your houses, and do not display your adornment as was the custom of jahiliyyah. This is a warning to safeguard them against worldly vanities.'

'Former times of ignorance' (al-jahiliyyatila-oola) refers to the culture of ignorance which the Qur'an was uprooting, and warns those who hoped to revive it that its era has passed.

'And establish prayer and give zakat and obey Allah and His Messenger' means focus on your spiritual development and spread this light within your homes, just as the Prophet spreads it outside.

'Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity, O people of the household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification' (Innama yureedullahu li-yudh-hiba 'ankumur-rijsa Ahlal-Bayti wa yutahhirakum tat-heera). 'Rijs' (impurity) refers to the vanity of worldly adornment, and Allah intends to purify the household of the Prophet from such impurity.

This verse highlights that:

First: A woman's primary sphere of action is her home, and she should limit her out-of-doors activities to cases of necessity under the established guidelines.

Second: Displaying adornment out-of-doors is a return to jahiliyyah, not a sign of progress.

Third: The term 'Ahlal-Bayt' (people of the household) refers primarily to the wives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon them), as established by this verse. Others are included only subordinately.

And remember what is recited in your houses of the verses of Allah and wisdom. Indeed, Allah is ever Subtle and Aware. (34)

This defines their mission: 'Remember and convey the verses and wisdom recited in your homes.' Since the Prophet's mission was to teach both men and women, and his private life was an essential part of his exemplary character, his wives were commissioned to preserve and convey these teachings, which they did with ultimate devotion. This was indeed one of the primary reasons why he was permitted more wives.

'Indeed, Allah is ever Subtle and Aware' (innallaha kana Lateefan Khabeera) means rest assured that He is fully aware of your efforts and needs within your homes, and He will reward you.

وقرن بیوتکن ولا تبرجن تبر الجاھلیۃ الاولی واقمن الصلوۃ واتینی الزکواۃ واطعن اللہ ورسولہ انما یرید اللہ لیذھب عنکم الرجس اھل البیت ویطھر کم تطھیرا (٣٣)
In this connection, these additional instructions are given, aimed at protecting the pure wives (may Allah be pleased with them) from the mischief of the hypocrites (male and female) and engaging them in tasks worthy of their status, for which Allah had chosen them. To understand the correct nature of these instructions, keep in mind what we pointed out earlier: that the hypocritical women were trying to create the same kinds of desires in the hearts of the Prophet's wives (ra) as they had in their own hearts. For example, they would say, "If you were to gain freedom from this confinement of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the great leaders of the time would send you marriage proposals, and you too would go out dressed up in adornments for shopping and sightseeing just as the wives of the wealthy do." Alluding to these behind-the-scenes whisperings and bad influences of the hypocritical women, the Quran urged the pure wives (ra) to stay quietly in their homes and not to go about displaying their adornments like the women of the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). This instruction is just like the guidance a well-meaning father gives to his obedient children when he learns that some wicked people are trying to mislead his children with bad advice. At that time, if he tells his virtuous son to focus all his attention on his education and not to wander the streets shouting slogans like the vagrants, it does not mean that his son was actually wandering like a vagrant or that there was an immediate fear of him becoming one. Rather, it is merely a rebuke to the vagrants of the time and a warning to protect his child from their ways. Similar guidance was given to the pure wives (ra). In reality, it is a critique of and disdain for the attitude of the ladies of that era. However, instead of addressing them directly, the address is directed at the wives of the Prophet (ra); otherwise, who among the Mothers of the Believers (ra) could even be suspected of having a desire to wander outside the home or display her makeup like the women of Ignorance?

Because of the adjective 'first' (Ula) associated with 'Ignorance' (Jahiliyyah), our commentators have taken it to mean the ignorance of the era of Prophet Noah (peace be upon him) or Prophet Idris. However, there is no need for such complexity. In our view, this is a highly eloquent way of pointing to the end of that ignorant civilization which the hypocrites (male and female) were still holding dear in their hearts, hoping that it would survive despite the blow it had received or was feared to receive from the emergence of Islam. To strike a blow to this hope of theirs, the Quran termed it 'the former ignorance' (Jahiliyyah al-Ula), meaning that you should now consider it a matter of the past—its era has ended, and those dreaming of its revival are living in a fool's paradise. Islam and Islamic civilization have hollowed out its roots, and if any of its remnants remain, they will soon be wiped out. Now, those who are guarding this house are guarding a ruined house. If they do not change their attitude, the day this house falls, they will also be buried under it.

'And establish prayer and give zakah and obey Allah and His Messenger.' That is, the work to be done is not what the ladies who champion the ignorant civilization are doing, but rather to establish prayer, pay zakah, and remain actively and fully loyal in obedience to Allah and His Messenger. Here, the reference to prayer, zakah, and obedience to Allah and His Messenger comes as comprehensive aspects of the religion. The meaning is that within your sphere of activity—namely, your homes—you should spread the same light that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions (ra) are spreading in public life.

'Allah intends only to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification.' Addressing the household (Ahl al-Bayt) with special care, this consoles them in a highly affectionate and loving manner, explaining that the purpose of these instructions is not to bind your lives in confinement, but rather that Allah wills to keep every impurity away from the household of prophethood and to train them in the highest manner to make them worthy of the companionship of His Messenger in both this world and the Hereafter. The word 'impurity' (rijs) here alludes to the adornments of those ladies, suggesting that this is not true adornment but a burden of filth they are carrying. Allah wills to keep the household of His Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) pure from this filth.

While the basic teaching of this verse has been explained above, a few other points are also worthy of attention.

First, a woman's primary sphere of activity is her home. She should keep her activities within its limits, and if she ever needs to step out of the house, she must adhere to the boundaries whose details will follow later in this Surah.

Second, women coming out of their homes adorned is not a sign of civilizational progress, but rather a regression to the former ignorance (Jahiliyyah al-Ula).

Third, the honor of being the Ahl al-Bayt (household of the Prophet) belongs primarily to his pure wives (ra). This verse stands as a definitive text (nass qat'i) on this matter. Parallels for this also exist in the Quran. There is no room here to interpret 'Ahl al-Bayt' as referring to anyone other than the wives of the Prophet (ra). If others are included, it is not primary but secondary and implicit. Therefore, the logic of those extremist sects who deny the primary application but raise a great storm over the secondary branches is beyond our understanding.

واذکرن مایتلی فی بیوتکن من ایت اللہ والحکمۃ ان اللہ کان لطیفا خبیرا (٣٤)
This explains to the wives of the Prophet (pbuh) their true purpose of life—that Allah has not chosen them for the things to which the hypocritical women are inviting them, but rather to propagate the teachings of the Quran and wisdom being taught in their homes. It should be remembered here that just as the Prophet (pbuh) was sent to guide men, he was also sent to guide women. Just as he taught people outside, he also taught within his homes. Revelation descended inside the home just as it descended outside. Furthermore, just as his every word was a source of teaching and guidance for the people, his every action inside the home was also an exemplary model for them. His life was not divided into separate private and public compartments; rather, every moment of his blessed life was dedicated to the education and training of the Ummah. For this reason, it was necessary that just as his devoted companions remained with him like a shadow to preserve every gesture of his public life, there should also be an arrangement to preserve every aspect of his life inside the home. This task, quite obviously, could only be accomplished through his pure wives (ra). Consequently, it is a historical fact that the knowledge and practice of the Prophet (pbuh) propagated through his pure wives (ra) is in no way less than the knowledge propagated through the companions (ra). This verse clearly shows that Allah Himself had commissioned his pure wives (ra) for this mission—that their job was not to collect the worldly trumpery but to distribute those treasures of knowledge and wisdom being showered inside their homes among the creation. In our view, this wisdom is also among those reasons for which the Prophet (pbuh) was specially permitted a number of wives. This issue will be discussed further.

'Indeed, Allah is ever Subtle and Acquainted.' That is, rest assured that if your duty is related to the inside of the homes, no service of yours will remain hidden from your Lord. God is highly observant of details and fully aware. He is well aware of your every action and your every need. Perform your duty trusting in Him; Allah Himself is sufficient for your needs.

ان المسلمین والمسلمت والمومنین والمومنت فالقتنتیں والقنتنت والصلقین والصدقت والصبرین والصبرات والخسعین والخشعت والمقتصدقین والمتصدقت والصالین والصئمت والخفظین نروجھم والحفظت والذکرین اللہ کثیر الذکرات اعد اللہ لھم مغفرۃ واجر اعظیما (٣٥)
The matters mentioned above in different places, some in negative and some in positive terms, are summarized at the end in a comprehensive style. The purpose is to show what qualities should characterize the society that is desired and beloved by Allah and His Messenger. This is, as it were, a mirror placed before Muslim men and Muslim women so that they may groom themselves accordingly and not be deceived by the hypocrites (male and female) who are seeking to smear their faces again with the darkness of ignorance which the Messenger of Allah had strived to wash away. These qualities are ten in number, and if we count the instructions scattered in the preceding section separately, they too will total ten. Since the address here is specifically to the pure wives (ra), and the objective is to state the constituent parts of Islamic society, the mention of women has not come implicitly but independently alongside men, because women constitute exactly a half and equal part of society, and their influence on the construction and ruin of society is perhaps slightly more than that of men.

The qualities listed here are as follows:
1. Islam, 2. Iman (Faith), 3. Qunut (Obedience), 4. Sidq (Truthfulness), 5. Sabr (Patience), 6. Khushu' (Humility), 7. Tasadduq (Charity), 8. Sawm (Fasting), 9. Chastity and Modesty (Hifz al-Furooj), 10. Remembrance of Allah (Dhikrullah).

These are ten in number. If we reflect, we will see that all aspects of Islamic morals and character are compressed within them. Since they are mentioned in the form of active participles (attributes), they are required as habits and permanent traits. This means that every individual of the society, whether man or woman, should be colored by them.

Among these qualities, some pertain to the rights of Allah (Huquq Allah), some to the rights of people (Huquq al-Ibad), and some primarily to self-reformation.

Islam and Iman are comprehensive expressions of the entire religion. Iman is the inner aspect of the religion, while Islam is its outer aspect, and both are required simultaneously.

The word 'Qunut' has passed above in verse 31. It refers to that obedience to God and the Messenger which is constant, born of complete focus of the heart, humility, and absolute sincerity.

'Sidq' is used to denote the consistency and firmness of word, deed, and intention. The words 'They were true to what they promised Allah' have passed in a preceding verse. From this perspective, it represents a character directly opposite to hypocrisy. In our language, the saying 'the word of a man is his life' expresses this very aspect.

'Sabr' stands for steadfastness, perseverance, firmness, and fortitude. A person who stands firm on the stance of truth against every fear and temptation, whether arising from within or without, and remains pleased and content with his Lord, is patient (Sabir), and indeed the entire religion stands on this character.

'Khushu' means humility and lowliness. This quality arises from a correct perception of the awe, majesty, and glory of God. This attribute bows a person before his Lord and makes him kind and gentle toward His creation. It is the opposite of pride (Istikbar), which is the root of all individual and social evils, whether related to the rights of Allah or the rights of His servants.

'Tasadduq' means giving charity. It is particularly related to the rights of the creation. When a person suppresses his own desires, curtails his own needs, and spends his wealth to meet the needs of others, it testifies to his faith in Allah and the Hereafter, and this gradually strengthens and solidifies his faith.

'Sawm' (Fasting) is a special discipline for self-control and training in patience. We have discussed its effects under Verses 183-187 of Surah Al-Baqarah. The foundation of all human character is patience, and fasting is the greatest means of training in patience.

'Hifz al-Furooj' (guarding the private parts) is an expression of chastity, which is the fruit of self-control. The most effective tool in the hands of Satan to cause corruption in society is to deaden the sense of chastity in men and women through propaganda. It has also passed in the verses above that the worst effort of the hypocrites (male and female) was to evoke a desire for displaying adornments in the pure wives of the Prophet (pbuh) so that the entire Muslim society might thereby embark on the path of immodesty.

'Dhikrullah' (remembrance of Allah) is the source and protector of all the aforementioned qualities. The more a servant remembers his Lord, the more these qualities become deep-rooted and firm within him. Indeed, the protector of the entire religion is the remembrance of Allah, and prayer is another name for the remembrance of Allah. Consequently, the mention of prayer as the protector of the entire religion and morality occurs repeatedly in the Quran, and we have discussed this.

6. The Next Subject... Verses 36-48
Next is an allusion to the event of Hazrat Zayd (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab (ra), which is actually a continuation of the preceding discourse. The hypocrites (male and female) had turned this event into a mischief, raising various questions. Allah answered all these questions, clarifying some highly important aspects of the religion. For example:
* The right of Allah and the Messenger is supreme; therefore, when the will of Allah and His Messenger becomes clear in any matter, a person's own choice ceases to exist.
* The duty of the Messenger is to call to and implement the commands of Allah; therefore, when a command is issued by Allah, it is his duty to execute it without caring for anyone's opposition or blame.
* Direct instruction to the Prophet (pbuh) to reform through his own action the custom regarding the wife of an adopted son that had persisted since the days of Ignorance, with a strong emphasis not to care about the clamor of the wicked.
* Since the Prophet (pbuh) is the Seal of the Prophets, it was necessary that every branch of the religion be completed at his hands, leaving no gap in any of its aspects.
* Mention of the great favor and blessing that Allah bestowed upon creation through the sending of the Prophet (pbuh), and an allusion to his rights over the Ummah.

Although these points are sufficient to understand the coherence and structure of this section, we will briefly mention the story of Hazrat Zayd (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab (ra) as well, so that there is no need to address the details under each verse.

Hazrat Zayd bin Harithah (ra) belonged to the tribe of Kalb. He was captured in childhood during a raid and made a slave. Hakim bin Hizam bought him for his paternal aunt, Hazrat Khadijah (ra). When Hazrat Khadijah (ra) was married to the Prophet (pbuh), she gifted him to the Prophet (pbuh). Thus, he had the honor of being in the service of the Prophet (pbuh). The value and respect of this service in his eyes can be judged from the fact that when his father and uncle came to the Prophet (pbuh) to demand his freedom, the Prophet (pbuh) gave him the choice to go with his father or to remain in his service. On this occasion, Hazrat Zayd (ra) set an example of love for the Prophet (pbuh) that will always be remembered in history. Despite being given the option of freedom, he preferred the service of the Prophet (pbuh) over that freedom. Perhaps Khwaja Hafiz wrote his matchless verse keeping this very incident in mind:
'By your love, if you call me your slave,
I will renounce the lordship of the universe.'

After this, the Prophet (pbuh) freed him. The Prophet (pbuh) loved him from the beginning because of his noble qualities, and after this incident, that love multiplied manifold—to the extent that seeing the Prophet's extraordinary attention and trust, people began to assume that he had made him his adopted son.

Events show that he also possessed administrative and military capabilities. On several occasions, the Prophet (pbuh) entrusted him with the command of military expeditions, and on some occasions, during the Prophet's absence, he served as the governor of Madinah.

To raise his social status, the Prophet (pbuh) married him to his first cousin, Hazrat Zaynab bint Jahsh (ra). She belonged to the family of Bani Asad, and her mother was Umaymah bint Abd al-Muttalib. When the Prophet (pbuh) proposed on behalf of Hazrat Zayd (ra) to Hazrat Zaynab (ra), her relatives objected to this match, on the grounds that Hazrat Zayd (ra) was a freed slave and not of equal status (Kufu). However, the Prophet (pbuh) wanted to change people's perceptions regarding slaves; hence, he insisted on this marriage. Ultimately, Hazrat Zaynab (ra) agreed, and the marriage took place.

After the marriage, the hypocrites (male and female) stirred up trouble, claiming that Muhammad (pbuh) had bound an honorable lady from a noble family to his freed slave. The public mind does not easily accept such social reforms, so a hostile atmosphere was created against this marriage. Especially the hypocritical women spared no effort in instigating Hazrat Zaynab (ra). They provoked her pride, saying it was a great injustice that she was married to a man who, until yesterday, was a purchased slave. After all, Hazrat Zaynab (ra) was human, not an angel, so these words must have had an effect on her heart.

Hazrat Zayd (ra) was a sensitive, self-respecting, and humble man. Despite all the kindness of the Prophet (pbuh), he had not forgotten his days of slavery. On the other hand, Hazrat Zaynab (ra) possessed a degree of natural dignity and temperament. In normal circumstances, this is not something that would disrupt a happy married life, but since the hypocrites had created an atmosphere of suspicion, Hazrat Zayd (ra) began to feel that Hazrat Zaynab (ra) had a sense of superiority over him and disliked this relationship. Eventually, he resolved to divorce Hazrat Zaynab (ra) so that her grievance could be resolved and his own burden could be lifted. However, before taking any step, he wished to know the inclination of the Prophet (pbuh) since he was the one who had arranged this marriage. When he mentioned his intention to the Prophet (pbuh), the Prophet asked if anything had appeared from her side that caused him doubt. He replied that there was absolutely nothing of the sort, but she was conscious of her family honor and expressed it, which caused him pain. The Prophet (pbuh) strictly forbade him from divorcing her and reminded him of the fear of God, because a mere personal feeling is not a valid reason to divorce a wife.

This intention of Hazrat Zayd (ra) became a source of concern for the Prophet (pbuh) for various reasons.

First, because the Prophet (pbuh) himself had arranged this marriage for a highly noble purpose, as mentioned above. Naturally, his desire was that, despite the machinations of the hypocrites, both parties should continue to live together happily and this relationship should succeed.

Second, this divorce would greatly damage the social status of Hazrat Zaynab (ra), doubling her grief. First, she had to hear the taunts of the hypocrites that she was the wife of a freed slave, and after this divorce, people would taunt her that she was the divorcee of a freed slave.

Third, the Prophet (pbuh) felt responsible for this entire situation; therefore, he considered it necessary to console Hazrat Zaynab (ra). The thought occurred to his heart that if Zayd (ra) divorced her, the only way to console Zaynab (ra) would be for the Prophet (pbuh) to marry her himself. However, in that case, there was an apprehension of a much larger mischief arising, as people would say that he (pbuh) had married the divorced wife of his adopted son. Furthermore, there was also the issue that the command restricting marriages to four had already been revealed for general Muslims, and at that time, the Prophet (pbuh) had four wives.

For these various reasons, the inner desire of the Prophet (pbuh) was that Hazrat Zayd (ra) should not divorce her; hence, he repeatedly urged him to abandon this intention. But Hazrat Zayd (ra) could not withstand the circumstances. It settled in his heart that this entire commotion had arisen because of his marriage, and the only remedy was to divorce her so that Hazrat Zaynab (ra) could be relieved of the distress and he himself could breathe a sigh of relief. Consequently, he divorced her. Hazrat Zaynab (ra) was deeply saddened by this divorce. It is mentioned in reports that when she was informed of it, she recited 'Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un'.

When matters reached this point, the Prophet (pbuh) wished to marry Hazrat Zaynab (ra), but he remained hesitant due to the custom of Ignorance regarding adopted sons and also due to the restriction on the number of wives. Finally, he was directed by Allah to marry her, regardless of the opposition of the people, so that a wrong custom could be reformed through his practice, and an unnatural restriction that had crept into the religion of nature could be brought to an end. In accordance with this divine instruction, the Prophet (pbuh) married Hazrat Zaynab (ra).

This is the actual event that I have placed before you after researching all reports. The need for this detailed explanation arose because orientalists have made this event look highly objectionable through their distortions, and sadly, they took all the material for these distortions from our own books of Tafseer and Seerah. Now, read these verses in this light.

٧۔ الفاظ کی تحقیق اور آیات کی وضاحت 
وما کان لمومن ولا مامنۃ اذا تضی اللہ ورسولہ امرا این یکون لھم الخیرۃ من امرھم ومن یعض اللہ ورسولہ فقد صل ضللا مبینا (٣٦)
This is the preface to the upcoming event, and the point stated herein is not confined to Hazrat Zayd (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab (ra) but has the nature of a universal principle. It was stated above in Verse 6: 'The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves.' In the light of this, this absolute principle was stated: that when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, there remains no room for any choice or objection for any believing man or believing woman. The Messenger of Allah decides only with the permission of Allah; therefore, his status is that of an absolute authority who must be obeyed. Then, it is not permissible for anyone to object to it or violate it. Whoever violates it falls into manifest error.

It should be kept in mind here that this ruling pertains to a situation where it is clearly known that what the Prophet (pbuh) has said is of the nature of a decisive command. Otherwise, there were occasions when he said something as a suggestion or advice, and when the companions (ra) came to know that it was not based on revelation but was the Prophet's own opinion or suggestion, they presented their own suggestions in comparison, and the Prophet (pbuh) sometimes accepted their suggestions.

From the details of the event of Hazrat Zayd (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab (ra) presented above, it is clear that neither of them opposed any decision of the Prophet (pbuh). When the Prophet (pbuh) proposed to Hazrat Zaynab (ra) on behalf of Hazrat Zayd (ra), she knew that this was not a command but a proposal in which she was free to express her liking or disliking. Later, when she realized that the Prophet's desire was that this match should take place, she accepted it. A similar situation arose when, after the divorce of Hazrat Zayd (ra), the Prophet (pbuh) proposed to Hazrat Zaynab (ra) for himself. She did not consider this as a command or decision but as a proposal, and said, 'I will not make any decision in this matter without seeking guidance (Istikhara) from my Lord.' Thus, she accepted it only after performing Istikhara.

Similarly, Hazrat Zayd (ra) did not violate any command or decision of the Prophet (pbuh). Regarding the advice not to divorce, he took the Prophet's statement as sincere advice and did not consider it a binding command. He felt that although the Prophet's desire was that divorce should not occur, the final decision of whether they could live together or not had to be made by him. Consequently, when he felt that it was no longer possible for him to maintain this relationship, he divorced her.

In our view, the nature of the ruling here is a general principle for which the circumstances of the time had prepared a suitable atmosphere. Through this, it became absolutely clear that it is not permissible for any believing man or believing woman to violate any command or decision of the Messenger (pbuh). This is contrary to the demands of faith, and whoever commits it commits open deviance. It was necessary to clarify this demand of faith to those hypocrites (male and female) whose attitude has been under discussion in this Surah from the beginning, so that they may unite in complete obedience to Allah and His Messenger, and if they are not ready for this, they should be ready to face the consequences.

واذ تقول للذی انعمل اللہ علیہ وانعمت علیہ امسک علیک زوجک واتق اللہ وتخفی فی نفسک ما اللہ مبدیہ وتخشی الناس واللہ احق ان تخشہ فلما قضی زید منھا وطرازوجنکھا لکی لا یکون علی المومنین حرج فی ازواج ادعیائھم اذ ققنوا منھن وطراط وکان امر امفعولا (٣٧)
This is a brief allusion to the actual event. Hazrat Zayd (ra) is mentioned here with the attribute: 'the one upon whom Allah bestowed favor and you bestowed favor.' There is a specific reason for mentioning him with this attribute here. It was mentioned above that the hypocrites (male and female) tried their best to lower him in the eyes of the people by taunting him with slavery so that they could succeed in their campaign. The pain this must have caused Hazrat Zayd (ra) is obvious, but he bore this insult with complete patience. Allah rewarded his patience by mentioning him with this attribute to make it clear to those who tried to insult him that the one they deemed low has been favored by Allah and His Messenger (pbuh). Obviously, one who is favored by Allah and the Messenger does not need the honor granted by anyone else.

Allah's favor is indeed upon every individual. Whatever a servant receives, he receives from God alone. However, if we reflect on the circumstances of Hazrat Zayd (ra), we find that his case was very similar to that of Hazrat Yusuf (peace be upon him). He was captured in a raid, remained a slave (most likely of a Christian), was sold as a slave, and finally, stage by stage, Allah through His providence brought him to the Prophet (pbuh), after which the doors of the blessings of religion and the world were opened for him.

The favor of the Messenger of Allah (pbuh) upon him was that through his service, he attained the blessing of Islam. The Prophet (pbuh) gave him a position of love and trust such that people began to consider him the Prophet's adopted son. The Prophet freed him from slavery and married him to his own cousin, which meant that the Prophet (pbuh) included him in his Ahl al-Bayt (household), and there can be no greater family honor than this.

Before 'Keep your wife to yourself and fear Allah,' the words 'And when you said' indicate that the Prophet (pbuh) said this to Hazrat Zayd (ra) repeatedly—to keep his wife in marriage and fear Allah. If it had been said only once, 'You said' (qulta) would have sufficed; 'You say' (taqulu) would not be necessary. This shows that Hazrat Zayd (ra) expressed his intention before the Prophet (pbuh) several times, and the Prophet stopped him each time and reminded him of the fear of God.

The words 'and fear Allah' suggest that the Prophet (pbuh) attributed Hazrat Zayd's intention of divorce merely to his intensity of feeling; he did not find any reasonable cause for this step. It has passed above that when the Prophet (pbuh) asked him, 'Has anything appeared from Zaynab's side that caused you doubt?' he clearly replied, 'There is absolutely nothing of the sort.' If he had any complaint, in his own words, it was only that 'she feels superior to me because of her noble lineage.' Obviously, this alone is not sufficient to divorce a wife. In this, the intensity of Hazrat Zayd's own feeling played a greater role than Hazrat Zaynab's attitude, especially in that atmosphere created by the hypocrites. For this reason, the Prophet (pbuh) reminded him of the fear of God so that he would not take any step overwhelmed by emotions but fear Allah. However, Hazrat Zayd (ra) was affected by the storm raised by the opponents, and his noble character and consideration for Hazrat Zaynab's feelings played a greater role in this. He felt that Hazrat Zaynab (ra) had become the target of criticism because of him, and the only remedy was to divorce her and rescue her from the long tongues of the critics.

'And you concealed within yourself that which Allah was to disclose, and you feared the people, while Allah has more right that you should fear Him.' This clause is in the state of circumstantial clause (hal), meaning: when you were saying to Zayd (ra) 'Do not divorce your wife, fear Allah,' at that time you were concealing in your heart a matter which Allah was going to disclose anyway.

Under this phrase, there is no need to address the absurd reports that our careless commentators have quoted. They are entirely baseless. Ibn Kathir's comment on them is perfectly correct: 'We preferred to turn away from them due to their lack of authenticity, so we do not cite them.' Our view regarding them is the same. We consider it a sin to quote them even for the sake of refutation. May Allah forgive Ibn Jarir (ra); he was highly careless in quoting reports.

The actual event is the one we pointed out above: since the Prophet (pbuh) himself had arranged this marriage under a religious wisdom, his inner desire was that it should succeed. Consequently, he tried to stop Hazrat Zayd (ra) with emphasis from his intention of divorce. He had the thought in his heart that if Zayd (ra) divorced her, Zaynab (ra) would suffer a double grief: she had already borne the taunts of enemies due to this marriage, and after this divorce, she would lose her social status forever by becoming the divorcee of a freed slave. The only way to console her and compensate for this loss was for the Prophet (pbuh) to marry her himself. However, in doing so, there was an apprehension that the hypocrites would make this a source of another and even greater mischief, spreading among the people that he (pbuh) had married the wife of his adopted son. He wanted to avoid this mischief; therefore, his inner desire was that divorce should not occur. But Allah's decision was that this should happen, so that a wrong custom of Ignorance could be reformed through his action, and that he should demonstrate through his practice that the prophets (peace be upon them) do not care for anyone's blame or opposition in the matter of religion.

The history of the prophets (peace be upon them) bears witness that they broke every stone in this path with their own hands and faced the greatest dangers for this purpose, leaving this model for those who would rise after them to serve the religion in their way.

'So when Zayd had no longer any need for her, We married her to you...' that is, the secret you kept in your heart but wished to avoid due to the fear of people's blame, Allah finally made arrangements for its disclosure. When Zayd (ra) severed his relationship with Zaynab (ra) completely, We married her to you so that the unnatural custom regarding adopted sons could be reformed, and no undue restriction would remain upon the people in this matter.

'Watar' means need or desire. From this, the concept of relationship and attachment also arises. Whatever a person needs, he naturally has an attachment to it. 'When Zayd had accomplished his desire from her' means Zayd severed his relationship with her completely. Instead of expressing this with the word 'divorce,' using this style has the benefit of pointing to the passing of the waiting period ('iddah), after the passage of which every relationship of the divorcing man with the woman is severed.

'We married her to you' means that while you wanted to avoid the responsibility of this marriage out of fear of the people, We, by Our command, married her to you. This shows that the Prophet (pbuh) performed this marriage under the instruction of Allah. It is mentioned in reports that after the waiting period passed, the Prophet (pbuh) sent the proposal to Hazrat Zaynab (ra) through Hazrat Zayd (ra) himself. She accepted it after performing Istikhara. Hazrat Zaynab's brother, Abu Ahmad bin Jahsh (ra), performed her marriage to the Prophet (pbuh), and the Prophet fixed four hundred dirhams as dower and arranged the walimah (wedding feast) with great care.
