Some people have understood from the word 'We married her' that this marriage took place in heaven and there was no need to perform the customary ceremony on earth. However, all those details are present in Ibn Hisham, which we have quoted above; therefore, the correct view is that this marriage took place by Allah's command in the well-known manner that Allah and His Messenger (pbuh) approved of for marriage.

'And the command of Allah is a decree determined.' That is, the time and method that Allah had appointed for the reform of this custom of Ignorance took place accordingly when the time arrived, and the obstacles placed by the enemies and the mischiefs raised by them could not disrupt this divine plan in the least.

ماکان علی النبی من حرج فیما فرض اللہ لہ سنۃ اللہ فی الذین خلوا من قبل وکان امر اللہ قدر مقدورا الذی یبلغون رسلت اللہ ویخشونہ ولا یخشون احد الا اللہ وکفی باللہ حسیبا (٣٨۔ ٣٩)
This is a consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) that the stages Allah has kept in your life's program are not for any distress, nor are they specific to you alone; rather, the previous prophets also had to pass through such stages. This is the sunnah (way) of Allah which He has established under His wisdom. The meaning is that the difficulties faced by the prophets are for their training and trial, and they occur exactly according to God's prescribed program. It is through these trials that their qualities are highlighted, and the sincerity or falsehood of their followers is also brought to light.

'Farada Allahu lahu' (Allah has ordained for him) is different from 'Farada Allahu 'alayhi' (Allah has obligated upon him). The meaning of the latter is that Allah has made it obligatory upon him, while the former means that Allah has destined for him that these states and stages will occur in his life.

In 'The way of Allah with those who passed before,' the linguistic style is similar to what has passed before. If we unpack this style, the full statement would be: 'Allah established that as a sunnah among the prophets.'

'And the command of Allah is a decree determined.' That is, Allah had pre-determined a time for the manifestation of His decision; when that time arrived, His command was issued. This shows that nothing in life happens by chance; rather, it happens according to the program designed by Allah. Therefore, one should neither be distressed by any matter assuming it to be a mere coincidence, nor ignore it, but rather understand it as coming from God and face it in a manner demanded by faith.

'Those who convey the messages of Allah and fear Him and do not fear anyone but Allah' is an attribute of the previous prophets—that they continued to convey the messages of Allah, and in this matter, they did not care for anyone else except Him. The meaning is that the responsibility Allah placed upon His previous prophets, which they discharged fearlessly without caring for any blamer, is the same He has placed upon you, and it is your duty to discharge it fearlessly like your predecessors.

In 'And sufficient is Allah as an accountant,' any interpretation of 'Haseeb' as 'Helper' and 'Supporter' is well-known. That is, Allah is sufficient to protect from all dangers. If this interpretation is accepted, the coherence of the text is perfectly clear. If 'Haseeb' is taken in its well-known meaning of 'one who takes account,' then this phrase would be of the nature of a warning—that if there is even the slightest shortcoming in conveying the message of Allah due to the fear or consideration of others, remember that Allah is sufficient to take account.

ما کان محمد ابا اھد من رجالکم ولکن رسول اللہ وخاتم النبین وکان اللہ بکل شئی علیما (٤٠ )
This is addressed to those who made the Prophet (pbuh) out to be the father of Hazrat Zayd (ra) to stir up trouble. It says: Muhammad (pbuh) is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Messenger of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets. In his capacity as a Prophet and Messenger, he has discharged the responsibility laid upon him by Allah, and since he is the Seal of the Prophets, it was necessary that this evil custom be reformed through him. If another prophet were to come after him, it was possible that Allah would defer this matter to the future, but now no prophet is to come; the completion of the religion must take place at his hands. Therefore, everything that is corrupted must be set right. The meaning is: do not look at his affairs in the light of your assumptions, but rather in the light of his actual office and its responsibilities. If you fail to recognize his actual office, you will have objections to many other things as well, whereas he must perform all those tasks fearlessly, for which he receives guidance from Allah. And remember that he is Allah's Messenger to you. If you deny him, you will not escape falling under that sunnah of Allah which He has established for those who deny His messengers, which has always manifested.

In 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men,' the denial is primarily of that misconception in which those people were involved who had made the Prophet (pbuh) the father of Hazrat Zayd (ra). However, it did not merely say 'Muhammad is not the father of Zayd,' but went further to say 'He is not the father of any of your men.' This made the statement more powerful and is also a statement of fact, because all of the Prophet's male children passed away in infancy; none of them reached the age of manhood.

'But he is the Messenger of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets.' We have discussed the difference between a Prophet (Nabi) and a Messenger (Rasool) in detail in various places in this book. Here, just remember that the relationship between Nabi and Rasool is that of general and specific. Every Rasool is necessarily a Nabi, but every Nabi is not necessarily a Rasool. Therefore, if the Prophet is the Seal of the Prophets (Khatam al-Anbiya), he is the Seal of the Messengers (Khatam ar-Rusul) by greater right. The issue raised by some deviant sects that the Quran describes the Prophet (pbuh) as Khatam al-Anbiya and not Khatam ar-Rusul, and therefore the continuation of the office of messengership is not negated, is merely a result of their ignorance.

'Khatam' and 'Khatim' are entirely synonymous according to the linguists. The last individual of a group, the end of a thing, the seal at the end of a letter—all these things are included in its meaning.

'And Allah is ever, of all things, Knowing.' That is, those who have raised all this clamor should know that Allah is more aware of all things than they are. He knows Zayd (ra), He knows Zaynab (ra), He is aware of His Prophet (pbuh), and He is aware of the nature of the relationship of Zayd (ra) and Zaynab (ra). He is not ignorant of any of these matters. Whatever has happened has happened with His permission and knowledge; therefore, raising a commotion against it is ignorance and folly. The demand of faith is to trust in Allah's knowledge and wisdom and to appreciate the reform that has taken place. Only God's all-encompassing knowledge can comprehend the subtleties and wisdoms of everything; others cannot encompass all His wisdoms.

یا یھا الذین امنوا ذکروا اللہ ذکر اکثیرا وسبحوہ بکرۃ واصیلا (٤١۔ ٤٢ )
This is an emphasis on the Muslims to remain steadfast in the face of this confrontation with the hypocrites and mischief-makers, and the remedy suggested is to remember Allah as much as possible and glorify Him morning and evening, ignoring the clamor of these wicked people. It should be clear that the believer's primary shield against Satan and his progeny is the remembrance of Allah. This is why, wherever steadfastness against opponents is urged in the Quran, prayer is emphasized in particular. We have been discussing the mutual relationship between patience and prayer in various places in this book (for example, see the commentary on Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 45).

In 'and glorify Him morning and evening,' glorification (tasbih) is an expression for prayer. This is the mention of the specific after the general. Remembrance is desired at all times like breathing, but Allah and His Messenger have appointed times for prayers, the comprehensive expression for which is morning and evening. Within this map of morning and evening, all the times of prayers are regulated, which we have explained in its place (see Surah Bani Isra'il, Verse 78; Surah Taha, Verse 130).

ھو الذی یصلی علیکم و ملئکتہ لیخرجکم من الظلمنت الی النور وکان بالمومنین رحیما (٤٣ )
Here, the blessing of remembering Allah abundantly is described: that He sends His mercy upon His believing servants, and His angels also constantly pray for mercy upon the believers. 'That He may bring you out from darknesses into the light' describes the blessing of this mercy—that it is by its virtue that He brings you out from darkness into light. Here, darkness refers to the darkness of beliefs and deeds, and light refers to the light of guidance and Shariah. 'And He is ever Merciful to the believers' is the attribute of Allah—that He is highly merciful to His believing servants.

In 'It is He who confers blessing upon you, and His angels,' the word 'yusalli' in relation to Allah means sending mercy, and in relation to the angels, it means praying for mercy. The change in the meaning of words with the change of relation is common in the Quran and Arabic speech. This very word is used in two different meanings in this very Surah: 'Indeed, Allah and His angels confer blessing upon the Prophet. O you who have believed, ask [ Allah to confer] blessing upon him.' At the end of the verse, 'And He is ever Merciful to the believers' also points to the fact that here this word 'yusalli' is used for Allah in the sense of sending mercy.

The mention of the angels' seeking forgiveness for the believers also occurs elsewhere in the Quran. For example:
'Those [angels] who carry the Throne and those around it exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and believe in Him and ask forgiveness for those who have believed, [saying], "Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge, so forgive those who have repented and followed Your way and protect them from the punishment of Hellfire."' (Al-Mu'min: 7)
Similarly, in Surah Ash-Shura:
'And the angels exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and ask forgiveness for those on earth.' (Ash-Shura: 5)

تحپتھم یوم یلقونہ سلم و واعڈ لھم اجرا کریما (٤٤ )
While the previous verse mentioned the mercy and blessings descended on the believing servants in this world, this verse mentions the greeting and message that will come to the believing servants from Allah and His angels in the Hereafter. It says: their greeting the Day they meet Him will be 'Peace,' and Allah has prepared for them a highly honorable reward.

The word 'Tahiyyah' here is annexed to its object, and its correct meaning is greeting or welcome. In other places in the Quran, it is also mentioned that the people of Paradise will receive greetings of peace from Allah, and the angels will also welcome them with peace.

In Surah Al-Furqan:
'And they will be met therein with greetings and peace.' (75)
In Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 58, it is also specified that peace will be conveyed to them from Allah Himself:
'[And] "Peace," a word from a Merciful Lord.'
In Surah Az-Zumar, Verse 73, it is specified that the angels guarding Paradise will welcome the people of Paradise with peace:
'And its keepers will say to them, "Peace be upon you; you have become pure; so enter it to abide eternally."'

یاکھا النبی انا ارسلنک شاہدا و مبشرا ونذیرا وادعیا الی اللہ باذنہ وسراجا منیرا (٤٥۔ ٤٦ )
This is addressed to the Prophet (pbuh), stating his office and pointing to the responsibilities associated with this office, so that it may become clear to the Prophet (pbuh) what tasks he has to perform and how to perform them, and so that the true nature of his personality may also become clear to others, allowing both supporters and opponents to review their respective attitudes in this light. It says: O Prophet! We have sent you as a witness, a bearer of glad tidings, and a warner. 'Witness' (Shahid) means the one who bears witness to the religion of Allah, His commands, and His pleasure. The primary purpose of sending a messenger is to tell his people what Allah has commanded and what He has forbidden.

'Bearer of glad tidings' (Mubashshir) and 'Warner' (Nadhir) mean that those who accept your message should be given the glad tidings of eternal success, and those who turn away or deny it should be warned of its consequences. After this style of warning and glad tidings, your responsibility regarding this witness ends. You bear no responsibility as to whether people accepted your call or rejected it, and if they accepted it, whether they accepted it with complete sincerity or with hesitation. All these matters rest with Allah.

'And one who invites to Allah, by His permission.' This is a further explanation of the same subject—that Allah, by His command, has commissioned you to call people toward Him, so that people may leave the path of Satan and his progeny and come toward their Lord. Along with this, the restriction 'by His permission' (bi-idhnihi) is for the consolation and reassurance of the Prophet (pbuh)—that since Allah Himself has commissioned you on this mission of calling to Allah, He will not leave you alone; rather, He will guide and help you at every step. The meaning is: you are not a self-proclaimed prophet that Allah would leave you to wander; rather, since Allah has placed this responsibility upon you, He will also help you in carrying it, and you will succeed in your mission despite all the hostile activities of the opponents.

'And an illuminating lamp' (Siraaj-an Muneer-an). That is, Allah has made you a bright lamp which is itself illuminated by the light of knowledge and wisdom and is also guiding people to bring them out of darknesses into Allah's straight path. If people do not appreciate this illuminating lamp, they will not harm you in the least; rather, they will only prepare their own ruin, wandering in misguidance in this world and becoming the fuel of Hell in the Hereafter.

وبشر المومنین بان لھم من اللہ فضلا کبیرا (٤٧ )
This clarifies the aspect of being a bearer of glad tidings: those who believe in your call should be given the glad tidings that they should not be frightened by the opposition of the opponents and the adversity of circumstances. If they remain steadfast in their faith, a great favor from Allah is going to descend upon them.

ولا تطع الکفرین والنفقین و دع اذنلھم و توکل علی اللہ وکفی باللہ وکیلا (٤٨ )
This clarifies the aspect of being a warner; however, instead of addressing the opponents directly to say something to them, Allah addressed His Prophet to tell him what attitude he should adopt toward these opponents in the future. In this attitude itself is implied that manner which indicates the fate these opponents are going to face in the near future. It says: do not pay any attention to the words of these disbelievers and hypocrites, ignore their annoyances, and trust in Allah; Allah is sufficient to rely upon. The consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) in this verse is evident from every word, and the anger and wrath for the opponents is also overflowing from every letter.

In 'and do not obey the disbelievers,' the word 'obedience' (ita'ah) is used here in the sense of paying attention to someone's words, giving them importance, and caring about them. The use of this word in this sense is well-known, and we have cited evidence in support of this meaning in its place.

In 'and ignore their harm,' the 'harm' (adha) refers to those annoyances caused by the hypocrites, such as they displayed in the matter of Hazrat Zaynab (ra), and had done before in the matter of Hazrat Aisha Siddiqah (ra). Further mention of their annoyances is coming up in this Surah. The word 'da' (ignore/leave) here is proof of disdain—that is, ignore these absurdities of theirs for a while.

In 'and rely upon Allah, and sufficient is Allah as a disposer of affairs,' there is as much consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) as there is a threat for the disbelievers and hypocrites—that now leave their matter to Allah; Allah is sufficient to deal with them!

٩۔ آگے کا مضمون۔ آیات ٤٩۔ ٥٢
The background of this collection of verses should also be kept well in mind, because there is great brevity in it, which has caused great confusion to the commentators.

These verses, in reality, serve as a refinement (istidrak) to Verse 3 of Surah An-Nisa, in which the command restricting the number of wives was stated for general Muslims. After the revelation of the said verse of An-Nisa, those Muslims who had more than four wives divorced the excess wives, but no divorce of any wife is proved from the Prophet (pbuh). The reason is that at that time, the Prophet (pbuh) had four wives in marriage. Later, when he married Hazrat Zaynab (ra) by the command of Allah, the hypocrites objected to this aspect as well, saying that he had made one Shariah for himself and another for others. To silence these hypocrites, Allah clarified the special permission given to the Prophet (pbuh) in the matter of wives. The prominent aspects of this special permission are:
* His wives whose dower he had paid were made lawful for him without exception.
* The captives (Ma Malakat Yaminukum) obtained as war booty; if he wished to free any of them and marry her, he could do so.
* Among the women of his close relatives, if any had migrated with him for the sake of religion, leaving her relatives and tribe, he could marry her.
* If any believing woman gifted herself to the Prophet (pbuh) and he wished to take her in marriage, it was permitted for him.
* Since these marriages were entirely for the welfare of the religion and the nation, the restrictions regarding marital rights that lay upon others were lifted from him. Along with these freedoms, two restrictions were also imposed on the Prophet (pbuh) which were not imposed on others:
  * One, that he could not marry outside this circle.
  * Second, that he could not replace these wives with other wives.

Understand also some basic points regarding the wisdom on which this freedom and restriction were based.

The Prophet (pbuh) performed all his marriages not for self-gratification but for the call of religion, reconciliation of hearts, consolation, and the welfare of the nation. You have read under Verse 24 that the mission with which Allah had charged him also involved the pure wives (ra) as partners; indeed, this was the primary purpose for which Allah had chosen them to accompany His Prophet (pbuh). The pure wives (ra) discharged this duty with great care and excellence, and our books of Hadith and Seerah bear witness to this. Especially the share of Hazrat Aisha (ra), Hazrat Umm Salamah (ra), Hazrat Hafsah (ra), and Hazrat Maymunah (ra) in this service is, if not more than that of the men, no less than theirs. The teachings of the Prophet (pbuh) regarding women spread mostly through these ladies (ra) and could only spread through them.

You have read the details of the circumstances and wisdom under which the Prophet (pbuh) married Hazrat Zaynab (ra). For the sake of an important social reform, she chose to make herself the target of criticism against the opinion of her brother and other relatives, but she did not reject the Prophet's word. If there could be any reward for her sacrifice in this world, it could only be that after Hazrat Zayd (ra) divorced her, the Prophet (pbuh) himself should honor her with marriage. Then, like her marriage to Hazrat Zayd (ra), her marriage to the Prophet (pbuh) also became a means of a great social reform, and in this too, she had to become the target of the absurd talks of the hypocrites (male and female), but she bore all these things for the sake of Allah and His Messenger.

Regarding the marriage of Hazrat Umm Habibah (ra) among the pure wives, the event is that she migrated to Abyssinia with her husband Ubaydullah, distressed by the persecution of the Quraysh. There, she faced the tragedy that after some time, her husband converted to Christianity. In that state of exile, she chose to separate from her husband and live a life of loneliness and helplessness, but she remained steadfast in her faith. In reward for her determination and steadfastness, the Prophet (pbuh) sent her the proposal of marriage. Anyone can estimate that if there could be any reward for this steadfastness in this world, it could only be that the Prophet should grant her this honor.

Regarding the Prophet's marriages to Hazrat Juwayriyah (ra) and Hazrat Safiyyah (ra), the nature is that they came into the Prophet's possession as war booty in the expeditions of Banu Mustaliq and Khaybar respectively. They were the daughters of chiefs. Keeping their family status in mind, the Prophet (pbuh) did not like to keep them as slave-girls; instead, he freed them and married them. The religious and political wisdom of these marriages is absolutely clear.

Some female companions (ra) only desired the honor of relationship with the Messenger of Allah (pbuh) and wished to gift themselves to him. Among such women, Hazrat Maymunah (ra) had the honor that the Prophet (pbuh) accepted her request, and reports indicate that the recommendation of the Prophet's uncle, Hazrat Abbas bin Abd al-Muttalib (ra), played a major role in the acceptance of her request.

The case of Hazrat Sawdah (ra) is similar. Her circumstances bear witness that she had no desire other than the relationship with the Prophet (pbuh).

From this detail, it is clear that the Prophet's case was not such that he should be bound by that restriction in marriage and divorce which was for general Muslims. Doing so feared the loss of many benefits; therefore, Allah revealed a regulation for him which had a degree of latitude so that those religious benefits could be maintained, and at the same time, it imposed some restrictions on the Prophet (pbuh) which were not on other Muslims, showing that the freedom granted to the Prophet (pbuh) was based entirely on wisdom. In this light, read the verses.

١٠۔ الفاظ کی تحقیق اور آیات کی وضاحت 
یایھا الذین امنوا اذا نکحتم المومنت ثم طلقتمو ھن من قبل ان تمسوھن مماکل علیھن من عدۃ تعدونھا فمتمعھن وسرحوھن سراحا جمیلا (٤٩ )
It will become clear from Verse 50 onwards that the clarification here is actually a refinement of the ruling given in Surah An-Nisa regarding restriction of marriages. Before the actual refinement, this verse serves as an introduction, answering an incidental question: if someone marries a woman but divorces her before the consummation of the marriage and meeting, there is no waiting period ('iddah) obligatory upon her. This question must have arisen particularly in that era because, in compliance with the command restricting marriages, cases of divorce must have occurred in abundance, and those divorcing must have thought it more appropriate to divorce those wives with whom marital relations had not yet been established. This verse provided them with an extremely just ease. Such people were directed that there is no need to observe the waiting period in the case of such women; rather, they should be sent off in an honorable manner, giving them something according to one's means. The details of this giving have passed in Verses 236-237 of Surah Al-Baqarah. There we have also clarified the importance of 'sending off in a beautiful manner' (sarah-an jameel-an)—that what is desired in Islam is that even if separation becomes necessary between husband and wife, it should be done as beautifully as possible, without causing any disgrace to either party.

یایھا النبی انا احللنا لک ازواجک التہ اتیت اجورھن وما ملکت یمینک مما افاء اللہ علیک و بنت عمک عمتک وبنت خالک وبنت خلتک التی ھاجرون معک وامراۃ مومنۃ ان وھبت نفسھا للنبی ان اراد النبی ان یستنکمھا خالصہ لک مندون المومنین قد علمنا ما فرضنا علیھم فی ازواجھم وما ملکت ایمانھم نکیلا یکون علیک حرج وکان اللہ غفورا رحیما (٥٠ )
We have pointed out above that when the command restricting the number of wives was revealed, indications show that the Prophet (pbuh) had four wives in marriage: Hazrat Aisha (ra), Hazrat Hafsah (ra), Hazrat Sawdah (ra), and Hazrat Umm Salamah (ra). Therefore, neither did the question of divorcing any wife arise for the Prophet, nor was there any room for criticism by the hypocrites or objectors. However, in 5 AH, when the Prophet (pbuh) married Hazrat Zaynab bint Jahsh (ra), the hypocrites raised a storm saying that he had married the wife of his adopted son, and also objected that he had made a separate Shariah for himself—restricting other Muslims to four wives but keeping himself above this restriction.

The details of Hazrat Zaynab's event have passed above, from which you have learned that the Prophet (pbuh) performed this marriage not by his own desire but by the command of Allah. But the objectors were not going to accept this; therefore, they must have raised objections. Ultimately, Allah explained in detail what privileges the Prophet (pbuh) possessed over other Muslims in the matter of marriage.

'O Prophet, indeed We have made lawful to you your wives to whom you have given their due compensation...' First, regarding the wives who were in your marriage at the time of the revelation of the verse, it says: We have made lawful to you your wives to whom you have given their dowers. 'Made lawful' does not mean that some prohibition had attached to them which was removed; rather, this was said to silence the hypocrites and objectors—that let them talk as they wish, do not care about them. Indications show that this verse was revealed in 5 AH. From the Makkan life until 5 AH, among the ladies who came into the Prophet's marriage, Hazrat Khadijah (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab bint Khuzaymah (ra) had passed away, while Hazrat Aisha (ra), Hazrat Hafsah (ra), Hazrat Sawdah (ra), and Hazrat Umm Salamah (ra) were alive. And the new addition was Hazrat Zaynab bint Jahsh (ra). Thus, regarding all of them, it was said that Allah made them lawful for you. They are introduced with the words 'to whom you have given their dowers' to distinguish them from the women mentioned next who were obtained as war booty, as the question of dower does not arise in the case of captives.

'and those your right hand possesses from what Allah has returned to you [of captives]...' That is, those captives obtained as war booty were also made lawful for you (pbuh) by Allah. Here, after 'those your right hand possesses,' the words 'from what Allah has returned to you' are particularly to be kept in mind. They show that ordinary slave-girls are not meant here, but rather those women who were captured in war. Among these captives, there were sometimes daughters and daughters-in-law of noble families and chiefs. The tradition of treating them with a degree of distinction existed in the days of Ignorance as well, and Islam maintained it; therefore, such women were usually given to the chiefs at the time of distribution. Hazrat Juwayriyah (ra) and Hazrat Safiyyah (ra) came to the Prophet (pbuh) in this manner during the expeditions of Banu Mustaliq and Khaybar respectively. He (pbuh) could have kept them as slave-girls, but he respected their family status, freed them, and married them. He performed these marriages under the special permission granted to him in this verse. If this permission had not been granted, he could have kept them as slave-girls but not as wives, because in that case, the number of his wives would have exceeded the general Islamic limit. Obviously, this would have been a kind of restriction that would interfere with some moral and political wisdoms related to his office; therefore, Allah permitted him to marry such women. These marriages took place in 5 AH and 7 AH respectively. Thus, by 7 AH, the number of his wives became seven.

'and the daughters of your paternal uncles and the daughters of your paternal aunts and the daughters of your maternal uncles and the daughters of your maternal aunts who emigrated with you...' This is a detail of the women of your closest relatives: among the daughters of your paternal uncles, paternal aunts, maternal uncles, and maternal aunts, if any has left her relatives and tribe for the sake of religion and migrated with you, and you wish to take her in marriage to appreciate and encourage her sacrifice, you may do so. In the command Allah gave to marry Hazrat Zaynab (ra), besides the reform of an evil custom of Ignorance, it was also kept in mind that she was his cousin and deserved consolation from the Prophet (pbuh) because of her sacrifices in the way of religion.

He also married the Mother of the Believers, Hazrat Umm Habibah bint Abi Sufyan (ra), under this permission. She was the daughter of his paternal uncle in relation, and the state of her sacrifices and bravery in the way of religion has been mentioned above.

Although no marriage of the Prophet (pbuh) is established among his maternal relatives, if there were any example like Hazrat Zaynab (ra) and Hazrat Umm Habibah (ra) among them, he could have married within that relationship under this verse.

'and a believing woman if she gives herself to the Prophet [and] if the Prophet wishes to marry her...' A woman gifting herself to someone means that she waives her every right and surrenders herself to him, being content and pleased with whatever marital rights he grants or waives. This is an extreme form of self-sacrifice, the desire for which, reports show, existed in several female companions (ra) merely to attain the honor of relationship with the Prophet (pbuh), and they expressed it to him. The Prophet's household life is known to everyone to be a life of poverty and hunger. You have read above that because of the simple life of the Mothers of the Believers (ra), the hypocritical women used to whisper to them that if they obtained a divorce, the great chiefs of the time would propose to them, and their life of poverty would be replaced by a life of luxury. For such a simple life, obviously, no woman could play this game for any worldly greed as to gift herself! This sacrifice could only be made by those women in whose hearts there was such a feeling of devotion and sacrifice for the Messenger of Allah (pbuh) that they were ready to sacrifice every desire of their lives to attain the blessing of his service. This was a highly pure desire; therefore, Allah protected it and permitted the Prophet (pbuh) that if any believing woman gifted herself in this manner and the Prophet (pbuh) wished to marry her, he could do so. From the restriction 'if the Prophet wishes to marry her,' it appears that although this desire is highly praised and pure, its encouragement is not highly possible for the Prophet (pbuh); therefore, this matter is left entirely to his discretion—whether he accepts such an offer or not. We have pointed out above that he accepted only one such offer, that of Hazrat Maymunah (ra). This marriage took place during the Umrah of Fulfilment (Umrat al-Qada) in 7 AH. After this, he did not perform any marriage.

'[this is] solely for you, excluding the [other] believers.' That is, this permission for more than four wives is specifically for you; it is not permitted for other believers.

'We certainly know what We have made obligatory upon them concerning their wives and those their right hands possess, [but this is for you] so that there will be upon you no discomfort.' This is an allusion to Verse 3 of Surah An-Nisa, in which general Muslims were restricted to not keeping more than four wives at a time. It says: what We have made obligatory upon general Muslims regarding their wives and slave-girls is before Us. Despite this, We are granting you this special permission so that no restriction remains upon you in this matter. That is, so that you may fulfill without any difficulty those religious and national benefits for which Allah has granted you this permission. We have pointed to these benefits above.

'And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful.' This is a consolation to the Prophet (pbuh) that although this permission has laid heavy responsibilities upon you regarding rights and duties, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful; He overlooks those shortcomings that occur without intention.

Based on some reports, some people have assumed that this verse was revealed after all the Prophet's marriages and served to reassure him that all the marriages he had performed were lawful. In our view, this is not correct.

First, after the clear command restricting marriages was revealed, how was it possible for the Prophet (pbuh) to perform any marriage contrary to this regulation without the permission of Allah? It was worthy of his status that he should be the foremost in acting upon this command.

Second, if the only purpose was to say that all your marriages are lawful, what was the need for this detailed description of relations and categories of women? Then, this short phrase would have been entirely sufficient: 'All the marriages you have performed so far have been made lawful for you by Allah. No one has any right to object to them.'

Therefore, this view is highly weak. In our view, the actual situation is the one we pointed to above: when the verse restricting marriages was revealed, the Prophet had four wives in marriage; therefore, the question of divorcing anyone did not arise. However, later, when he married Hazrat Zaynab (ra), this became a cause of objection for the objectors, and this objection could also create doubts in other well-meaning people. To prevent this possibility, Allah explained in detail that he married Hazrat Zaynab (ra) by the command of Allah, and that the Prophet (pbuh) has a separate regulation from general Muslims in this matter. He can marry women of such-and-such categories in the future as well. Thus, Hazrat Zaynab's marriage was made lawful, and a regulation was also established for the future. In short, in our view, this verse was revealed in 5 AH after the marriage of Hazrat Zaynab (ra).

ترجی من تشاء منھن وتوی الیک من تشاء ومن ابتغیت ممن عذلت فلا جناح علیک ذلک ادنی ان تعرا حینھن ولا یحزن ویرضین بما اتینھن کلھن واللہ یعلم فی قلوبکم وکان اللہ علیما حلیما (٥١ )
It has been explained in detail above that all these marriages of the Prophet (pbuh) were primarily for the welfare of the religion; therefore, just as he was exempted from the general regulation in the matter of the number of wives, he was also given freedom in the matter of marital rights—leaving it entirely to his discretion. You may defer whom you wish of them and take to yourself whom you wish. Although the matter of justice between wives is of great importance, Allah exempted him from this restriction as well, for two reasons.

First, the one to which we pointed above: that in the Prophet's marriages, the general purpose of marriage was entirely secondary and auxiliary. The primary purpose was religion, and for this purpose, there is no need for those restrictions and boundaries that are appointed for ordinary couples.

Second, there was no apprehension regarding the Prophet (pbuh) that he would make wrong use of this freedom. Consequently, all his biographers are agreed that despite this freedom, he maintained complete justice upon himself and never violated it until the end.

'And him whom you desire of those you had set aside—there is no blame upon you [in returning her].' That is, you are also permitted that if you wish to keep any of your wives completely set aside from marital relations and then restore relations with her, you may do so. In this matter too, there is no restriction upon you, nor will any of your wives have the right to object to it.

'That is more suitable that they should be content and not grieve and that they should be satisfied with what you have given them—all of them.' This is an admonition and encouragement to the pure wives (ra) that they should not evaluate their relationship with the Prophet (pbuh) on the touchstone of general husband-wife relationships. Rather, they should evaluate it keeping in mind the primary responsibility of the Prophet (pbuh) and their own true status. The main thing is the perspective. If this changes and they understand that their primary relationship with the Prophet (pbuh) is not merely that of husband and wife but of the service of religion, then there will be no rivalry between the wives regarding rights, nor will there be any complaint against the Prophet (pbuh); rather, they will be content with whatever of his busy moments the Prophet (pbuh) grants to them. After the change of perspective, the one whose status is higher in terms of religious service will be appreciated by the Prophet (pbuh) and also in the eyes of his other wives, and all the bitterness of mutual jealousy and rivalry will vanish.

'And Allah knows what is in your hearts. And Allah is ever Knowing and Forbearing.' This is a reminder to the Prophet (pbuh) and his wives that everyone should keep in mind the reality that Allah knows even the secrets of the hearts, but along with knowing, He is Forbearing and Tolerant; therefore, He also overlooks. Keeping in mind this knowledge and forbearance of Allah creates that true concept of fear of God and His forgiveness in man which maintains a balance between hope and fear, establishing man's life on the correct path.

لا یعدلک النساء من بعد ولا ان تبدل بھن من زواج ولاعجبک حسنھن الا ما ملکت یمینک وکان اللہ علی کل شئی رقیبا (٥٢ )
Aside from the categories of women mentioned above, all other women were made unlawful for the Prophet (pbuh). Ibn Kathir (ra) has quoted the opinion of a whole group, including leading commentators like Ubayy bin Ka'b, Mujahid, Ikrimal, Dahhak, Abu Razin, Abu Salih, Hasan, Qatadah, and Suddi (ra): 'And aside from them, other categories of women are not lawful for you.' A specific circle mentioned in Verse 50 was designated for the Prophet (pbuh), outside of which he could not marry.

'nor that you should replace them with other wives, even if their beauty were to please you...' And you were also not permitted to divorce any of these wives to bring another wife in her place, even if she pleased you. After this, only the permission for captives (Ma Malakat Yamin) remained; consequently, under this permission, Hazrat Mariyah (ra) came into his possession, from whose womb the Prophet's son, Hazrat Ibrahim, was born.

'And Allah is ever, over all things, an Observer.' This verse is obviously of the nature of reminder and warning, and it is also obvious that the addressee here is the Prophet. In the preceding verse, you saw that a similar reminder was addressed to the pure wives (ra). This shows that everyone is accountable before Allah, and the greater one is, the more accountable he is. Therefore, it is necessary for everyone to keep accounting of himself before God's reckoning, and to do this with the conviction that no corner of his life is hidden from God's eyes.

If we reflect on this freedom and restriction explained for the Prophet (pbuh) in the verses above, we will find that although he was freed from the restriction of four in the matter of wives, on the other hand, the restrictions imposed upon him were such that he was much more restricted than other Muslims in both marriage and divorce.

A general Muslim has the freedom that, keeping the limit of four, he may marry any woman he wishes outside the prohibited degrees, and divorce any of his wives to bring another wife of his choice in her place. But the Prophet (pbuh) did not have this freedom. After the revelation of this command, he could marry only from three categories of women:
* A woman from his closest relatives who had migrated with him.
* A woman obtained as war booty in some jihad.
* A woman who gifted herself to the Prophet (pbuh) and the Prophet (pbuh) wished to marry her.

Outside this circle, he could neither marry, nor could he make any alteration in these wives merely on the basis of liking or disliking. The wisdom on which this permission was based has been explained above.

١١۔ آگے کا مضمون۔ آیات ٥٣۔ ٦٢
Next, to prevent the machinations and annoyances of the hypocrites, some instructions regarding hijab (veiling) are given to the wives of the Prophet (pbuh) and also to the wives and daughters of general Muslims. Some rulings regarding hijab have also been explained in Surah An-Nur. In this Surah, this chapter is completed so that no loophole remains for the intrusion of the wicked and mischief-makers. At the end, the hypocrites are threatened that if they do not desist from their mischiefs, such commands will be issued concerning them that it will be impossible for them to find shelter in this land... In this light, read the verses.

١٢۔ الفاظ کی تحقیق اور آیات کی وضاحت 
یایھا الذین امنو تلا تدخلو بیوت النبی الا ان یوذن لکم الی طعاف گیر نظیرین اتہ ولکن اذدعیم فادخلوافاذطعمتم فانتشروا ولا مستانسعین لحدیث ان ذلکم کان یوذی النبعی فیسحی منکم واللہ لا یستحی من الحق، واذا سالتموھن متاعا فسئلوھن من ورا حجاب، ذلکم اطھر لقلوبک وقلوبھن وما کان لکم ان توذو ارسول اللہ ولا ان تنکحوا ازواجات من بعدہ ابدا ان ذلکم کان عند اللہ عظیما (٥٣ )
The necessary etiquettes regarding entering and leaving each other's houses have been explained in Surah An-Nur. Here, those etiquettes are further explained regarding the Prophet's homes, and the need for this further explanation arose because of those hypocrites whose attitude has been under discussion in this Surah. The Prophet (pbuh) would occasionally invite the companions (ra) to his home for food on some occasion. On such occasions, he would also invite those who were involved in hypocrisy out of his noble nature and to reconcile their hearts. And even if he did not invite them, some of them would arrive as uninvited guests. These people would take wrong advantage of the Prophet's noble nature. First, they would occupy the place long before the time of the feast, and then, after eating, they would not think of leaving but would remain engaged in talk. They would commit further mischief by entering before the pure wives (ra) under the pretext of asking for something. Their purpose in all these actions was the one we pointed to earlier: to find some opportunity for whisperings and machinations among the pure wives (ra). The Prophet (pbuh) would perceive these actions of theirs, and they caused him pain, but he would ignore them out of consideration and modesty. But there is a limit to ignoring. When it became clear that these people did not deserve this noble treatment, Allah gave highly clear commands in this regard and also warned them that if they did not change their ways, they would be inviting their final decree.

'O you who have believed, do not enter the houses of the Prophet except when you are permitted for a meal, without awaiting its readiness.' Although the address is general so that the benefit of this teaching remains general, behind the scenes are those very hypocrites to whom we pointed. Allah did not wish to expose them at this stage; therefore, the statement is made in a general form. It says: O believers, do not enter the homes of the Prophet (pbuh) unless you are permitted for a meal. Two things are implied in the style of 'unless you are permitted for a meal': first, do not enter the homes without permission, and second, do not arrive at a feast uninvited. 'without awaiting its readiness' is the third condition. 'Nazireen' means waiting, and 'anahu' means its readiness and cooking time. That is, it is also not permissible that because you have an excuse of a feast, you should go and sit there waiting for the food to be prepared. First, this is proof of greed and lowliness. Second, in those days, general houses did not have separate drawing rooms next to the women's quarters. The same was the case with the Prophet's homes. Therefore, the gathering of people before the time was a source of trouble and annoyance for the family and the host.

'But when you are invited, then enter; and when you have eaten, disperse without seeking to remain for conversation.' This is the correct method prescribed: when you are invited, enter at the time, and when you have eaten, disperse; do not remain sitting there engaged in talks. 'But when you are invited' unfolds the meaning implied in 'except when you are permitted for a meal.' That is, one should go only when invited; one should not try to become an uninvited guest. 'without seeking to remain for conversation' is an instruction exactly like the one given in 'without awaiting its readiness.' That is, just as it is disliked that you should go and sit beforehand waiting for the food to be prepared, it is also disliked that you should remain sitting there engaged in talk after eating.

'Indeed, that [behavior] was troubling the Prophet, and he is shy of [dismissing] you. But Allah is not shy of the truth.' 'That' refers to all those behaviors mentioned above. It says: these things were a source of trouble and annoyance for the Prophet (pbuh), but he would not check you out of modesty and consideration, but Allah is not shy of the truth; therefore, He is informing you of these things. It should be kept in mind that Allah has not mentioned here any weakness of the Prophet (pbuh), but has pointed to a commendable trait of his—that he is highly noble and considerate; therefore, Allah has warned the people of their mistakes, and it became necessary for the Prophet to announce them, leaving aside modesty and consideration.

'And when you ask [his wives] for something, ask them from behind a partition.' The pronoun refers to the Prophet's wives (ra). The houses of the Prophet have been mentioned above; therefore, a highly clear clue was present to refer to the Prophet's wives (ra). It says: if anyone needs to ask them for something, he should not enter before them but ask from behind a curtain. 'That is purer for your hearts and their hearts.' This is a refutation of an implied objection. The meaning is: apparently, it seems to be an unnecessary formality that if someone needs to ask them for even a glass of water, he should arrange for a curtain, but this is not a formality; rather, it is a highly necessary measure to protect the heart from afflictions. The One who made the human heart is well aware of its weaknesses. He knows through which hidden ways this heart receives bad influences, and the heart is that on which the entire moral health of man depends; therefore, it is necessary that those who desire the health of their hearts should protect it from all those things that can make it dusty. The champions of civilization of this era pay great attention to the cleanliness of their clothes—they would not let a crease or a stain fall on them—but they do not care if their hearts remain smeared with filth. The reason is that moral and spiritual health has no importance in their eyes, whereas the primary importance belongs to this. The Prophet (pbuh) pointed to this reality in these words: 'Indeed, in the body there is a piece of flesh; if it is healthy, the whole body is healthy, and if it is corrupt, the whole body is corrupt. Indeed, it is the heart!' The more delicate and valuable things are in the human body, the more they have to be protected. The heart is the most valuable and the most sensitive; therefore, Allah made the greatest arrangement for its protection.

'And it is not [lawful] for you to harm the Messenger of Allah or to marry his wives after him, ever.' It says: it does not behove you to cause annoyance to the Messenger of Allah by your behaviors mentioned above, nor is it permissible for you to marry his wives (ra) after him, ever.

This phrase has lifted the veil, through the principle of gradual disclosure, from the faces of those people keeping whom in view these commands are given. Obviously, they were the hypocrites who did such things to cause annoyance to the Prophet (pbuh), and they were the ones who kept the desire in their hearts to marry his wives so that they could make it a source of mischief against Allah and the Messenger. The style of this verse is of warning; therefore, the Prophet (pbuh) is mentioned here with the word 'Messenger of Allah,' whereas in the preceding verses he was repeatedly mentioned with the word 'Prophet.' The reason is to show the intensity of this warning, because a messenger, as we have repeatedly clarified in this book, is God's court for his people. When he comes, he does not come merely to deliver a sermon, but to decide between the good and the bad of the nation. Therefore, mocking him or causing him annoyance is not a child's play. The meaning is: those who are playing this game are playing with fire. They should keep its end before them.

'nor to marry his wives after him, ever.' This has also struck the final blow to their desire to marry the Prophet's wives (ra) which they kept in their hearts. It says: it is not lawful for you to marry the Prophet's wives (ra) today or ever. It has passed in Verse 6 that the status of the Prophet's wives is that of the Mothers of the Ummah, and this is the natural and rational nature of relationship. Natural because for the Prophet's wives, every member of the Ummah (if he has a spark of faith) has a feeling of respect and devotion much greater than for his own mother. With this feeling, any member of the Ummah would feel the thought of marrying them much more heavy and shameful than the thought of marrying his own mother. If a desire to marry them arises in anyone, it means that his faith has become weak and his nature has become corrupt.

Rational because the status of the Prophet's wives, as passed in Verse 34, is that of teachers for both men and women of this Ummah, and Allah Himself has commissioned them for this office. The demand of this office is that they be placed in the status of mothers. Only by remaining in this status can they maintain their true dignity, and only then can others derive benefit from them in the manner that should be derived from the teachers of the Ummah. If they are lowered from this status, neither can they maintain their true dignity, nor can others derive benefit from them in the correct manner.

ان تبدوا شیاء اوتخفوہ فان اللہ کان بکل شئی علیما (٥٤ )
'Indeed, that was in the sight of Allah an enormity.' This is an admonition, and a highly severe one. It says: these things are highly grave in the sight of Allah. Here, He did not specify the punishment, and the threat and intimidation implied in this ambiguity is obvious.

'Whether you reveal a thing or conceal it, indeed Allah is ever, of all things, Knowing.' This is also an admonition reinforcing the warning above. The meaning is: since God is aware of even the secrets of the hearts, nothing can be hidden from Him. In this world, one can coin highly beautiful excuses for one's improper actions, but these excuses will not work before God. He will place even the hidden corruptions of the hearts before everyone.

لا جناح علیھن فی ابائھن ولا ابنائھن ولا اخوانھن ولا ابناء اخوانھن ولا ابناء اخواتھن ولا نسائھن ولا ماملکت ایمانھن واتقین اللہ ان اللہ کان علی کل شئی شھیدا (٥٥ )
This verse is in the same context as Verse 31 of Surah An-Nur: 'and not display their adornment except...' Most of the words are common to both. Above, in the verse concerning the homes of the Prophet's wives, some restrictions were imposed on others entering their homes. This is a detail of those relatives who are exempt from these restrictions. The close relatives have been mentioned, and the purpose is that all those relatives who fall under their category are exempt from this restriction. Upon them, only those restrictions will lie which have been mentioned in Surah An-Nur.

"And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is ever over all things a Witness." This is a warning given at the end to the wives of the Prophet (ra) as well, similar to the warnings given to others above. The meaning is that the purpose of these commandments and guidance is not mere lip service, but that they must be implemented with complete God-fearingness and piety, keeping the reality always present in the heart that Allah is everywhere present and watching. There is no place where a human can hide from God. "Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet. O you who have believed, ask [Allah to send] blessings upon him and ask [Allah to grant him] peace." (56)
Instead of causing distress and harm to the Prophet (pbuh), this is guidance on the attitude that believers should adopt towards him. It states that Allah sends His mercy upon His Prophet (pbuh), and His angels also pray for mercy upon him. Therefore, the correct approach for believers, in harmony and alignment with God and His angels, is also to send abundant blessings and peace upon the Prophet (pbuh) rather than plotting to cause him distress. 
Several realities emerge from this verse that are worth keeping in view:
First, for a Prophet (pbuh) whose stature in the sight of Allah and His angels is such that Allah sends mercy upon him and the angels constantly pray for mercy upon him, it is a matter of great pity if some humans seek to harm him, especially when the Prophet's (pbuh) primary favor is upon humans, not upon God or His angels.
Second, those who send blessings and peace upon the Prophet (pbuh) do not do him any favor. Rather, by harmonizing with God and His angels, they make themselves deserving of mercy. As far as the Prophet (pbuh) is concerned, since he enjoys the mercy of Allah and the prayers of the angels, he is not in need of others' prayers.
Third, sending blessings and peace upon the Prophet is a remedy for the disease of hypocrisy. This is because the context in which this guidance is given suggests that believers should send blessings upon the Prophet instead of causing him distress like the hypocrites. This clearly implies that hypocrisy does not find a way into those who diligently send blessings upon him.
Fourth, the objective is the abundance of sending blessings and peace. The context demands this meaning, and the words of the verse also testify to it. This is because in 'tasliman,' the absolute object (masdar) indicates emphasis and abundance. Therefore, we do not agree with the opinion of those jurists who say that if someone recites blessings once in a lifetime, the right of this verse is fulfilled.

"Indeed, those who abuse Allah and His Messenger - Allah has cursed them in this world and the Hereafter and prepared for them a humiliating punishment." (57)
This describes the end of those who were guilty of hurting and insulting the Messenger of Allah. It is stated that causing distress to the Messenger is, in fact, causing distress to Allah Himself. The Messenger is the beloved and representative of God, and since God and His angels send mercy upon him, those whose attitude is contrary to this are necessarily opponents of God and His angels, and are deemed as causing distress to God. As a retribution for this act of theirs, Allah's curse is upon them in both this world and the Hereafter. They will be disgraced in this world, and a humiliating punishment is prepared for them in the Hereafter. It is particularly noteworthy here that they have been threatened with punishment in this world even before the punishment of the Hereafter. This points to the Sunnah (way) of Allah that has been established for the opponents of the Messengers. Its explanation follows in verses 60–61. This is precisely the same punishment that was meted out to the Jews, whose primary crime was also that they caused distress to the Messengers of Allah. Further explanation of this will, God willing, be provided in the commentary of Surah As-Saff.

"And those who harm believing men and believing women for [something] other than what they have earned have certainly born upon themselves a slander and manifest sin." (58)
In the preceding verses, the attitude of the hypocrites specifically toward the Prophet (pbuh) was discussed. Now, this points to the mischief they were committing to slander the general believing men and believing women. We have explained in detail in the commentary of Surah An-Nur that it was highly painful to the hearts of these hypocrites that Muslims possessed an extraordinary moral superiority over all other groups, which acted as a major factor in the popularity of their message. Out of intense envy, they devised a counter-strategy to fabricate baseless allegations against Muslim men and women and spread them among the people so that the moral reputation of Muslims would be damaged and their own malice satisfied. The details of the Incident of Slander (Ifk) have been explained in the commentary of Surah An-Nur; that fitnah was also initiated for this very purpose. Here, Allah refers to this failed attempt of theirs, stating that those who implicate Muslim men and women in false accusations are committing a blatant slander and an open sin. The meaning is that if they do not desist from this approach of theirs, they will face the consequences!

"O Prophet, tell your wives and your daughters and the women of the believers to draw their cloaks close over themselves. That is more suitable that they will be known and not be abused. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful." (59)
To protect them from the harassment of the hypocrites and evil-doers, both the household of the Prophet (pbuh) and the general Muslim women were instructed that when they need to step out of their homes for any necessity, they should draw a part of their outer cloaks (jalabib) over their faces. In this way, a distinction would be made between them and non-Muslim women or slave-girls, leaving no excuse for anyone to harass them.
In Surah An-Nur, it will be recalled, guidance was given regarding close relatives that when they enter homes after permission, the women of the house should remain modest, not display their adornment, and draw their veils over their chests. Here, the guidance is that they should draw a part of their large cloaks (jalabib) over themselves. In other words, they should draw a veil (ghoonghat) using these large cloaks. This is a clear indication that this guidance relates to the situation when women have to go outside for some necessity. One proof of this indication is that its benefit is stated as: 'That is more suitable that they will be known and not be abused.' Obviously, this objective can only be relevant when going outside. Another clear indication is that the word used here is not 'khimar' (veil) but 'jilibab' (outer cloak). Lexicographers explain 'jilibab' as: 'It is the cloak worn over the khimar.' 'Jilibab' refers to the large sheet or cloak taken over the head-covering. Naturally, the need to wear this large cloak did not arise inside the homes; rather, the women of noble Arab households wore it when they needed to go out. This custom of 'jilibab' among noble Arabs is proven by the poetry of the Jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic) period. While there is no room to quote many verses here, a single verse by a poetess of the Hudhayl tribe is sufficient for our argument. In an elegy for a slain relative, she says:
'She walks gracefully and lightheartedly, like the walk of young maidens wearing jalabib.'
The rare metaphor in this verse is not the subject of discussion here, as it would lead us off track. Just remember that in the Jahiliyyah era, not all women were unrefined; there were also daughters and daughters-in-law of noble families who would put on a jilibab over their head-coverings when going outside. The Qur'an commanded regarding this jilibab that when Muslim women go outside their homes, they should draw a part of it over themselves so that the face is also covered to some extent, and it does not hinder them in walking. This same 'jilibab' is still prevalent among the respectable elderly women in our villages, and with modern fashion, it has now taken the form of the burqa. Modernists of our time may declare this burqa contrary to civilization if they wish, but its command is present in very clear terms in the Holy Qur'an, which can only be denied by those self-deluded individuals who claim to be more civilized than God and His Messenger.
'That is more suitable that they will be known and not be abused.' This was established as a distinguishing mark for Muslim women so that wherever they went, everyone would recognize them from afar as Muslim women and not dare to harass them—or if they did, they would think twice about the consequences. In that era, there were also non-Muslim women in Medina who did not observe the veil, as well as slave-girls whose standard of living and moral conduct were low. This gave evil-minded people many excuses. If they passed a remark at a Muslim woman on the street or tried to approach her and were caught, they would make the excuse: 'We thought she was so-and-so's slave-girl, and we only wanted to ask her about something.' Once this distinguishing mark was established, the way for such excuses was blocked.
Let no one misunderstand this passage to mean that it was a temporary measure adopted solely to protect Muslim women from the evil of troublemakers, and that its necessity no longer remains. First of all, all commandments were revealed under certain circumstances, but this does not mean that if those specific circumstances change, the commandments become void. Secondly, regarding the conditions in which this commandment was given, can any rational person claim that today's conditions are not a thousand times worse than those of yesterday? However, the concepts of modesty and chastity taught by the Qur'an have largely vanished.
'And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful.' This phrase is revealed to offer ease and comfort. The meaning is that women should observe this precaution to the best of their ability. If, despite this, any lapse or oversight occurs, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

"If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease and those who spread rumors in Al-Madinah do not cease, We will surely incite you against them; then they will not remain your neighbors therein except for a little. Accursed wherever they are found, they shall be seized and massacred completely." (60–61)
This is a warning to those troublemakers and hypocrites that if they do not desist from their mischief, the attitude of tolerance and overlooking that has been shown to them so far will be completely changed, and We will command you (the address is to the Prophet [pbuh]) to call them to account and pursue them at every step. Then they will find very little time left to live in this city. After that, for whatever days they survive here, they will live as accursed ones. They will be apprehended at every turn. They will be captured and executed in highly exemplary ways.
In 'and those in whose hearts is disease,' the word 'disease'—as explained in other contexts—refers to envy, malice, and deep-seated animosity. This group of hypocrites was not merely suffering from weakness of will or resolve; rather, they harbored intense envy against the Prophet, Islam, and the Muslims. However, out of cowardice, instead of opposing openly like the disbelievers, they infiltrated the ranks of Muslims to try to harm Islam and Muslims from within.
'Those who spread rumors in Al-Madinah (Al-Murjifun)' refers to that group of hypocrites who were highly adept at spreading panic and false rumors in Medina. Their objective with these rumors was to demoralize the Muslims and damage their moral standing. The role of this group discussed earlier in relation to the Battle of the Trench and the matter of Hazrat Zayd (ra) and Hazrat Zaynab (ra) is a very clear example of this 'irjaf.' Lexically, 'irjaf' means propagating bad and mischievous news with the intention of spreading anxiety and unrest among the people. This shows that the experts of 'irjaf' are not a product of this era alone; rather, major leaders of this craft have existed in the past as well.
The literal meaning of 'lanughriyannaka bihim' is 'We will certainly incite you, provoke you, or urge you against them.' Its import is that until now, We had instructed you to adopt a course of pardon and tolerance regarding them, but instead of appreciating this, they are growing bolder day by day. If they do not desist from this mischief, We will give you the commands to apprehend them, after which the land of Medina will become too narrow for them. Firstly, they will barely be allowed to remain in your vicinity of peace, and even if they do, it will be with Allah's curse and rejection. 'Wherever they are found, they shall be seized and massacred completely' is a vivid depiction of this accursed state. Consequently, in the later periods, these hypocrites faced this very end. Those among them who did not reform their ways met the same fate as the wicked among the Quraysh and the Jews. The details of this have already been mentioned in Surah Al-Anfal and Surah At-Tawbah.

"[This is] the established way of Allah with those who passed on before, and you will not find in the way of Allah any change." (62)
This is precisely the same style of discourse that occurred in verse 38. The meaning is that those among the hypocrites and disbelievers who opposed the Prophets in the past were dealt with by Allah in the same manner. Keep this Sunnah (way) of Allah in view; Allah will deal with these enemies of yours in the same way. There is never any change in the Sunnah of Allah.

13. Subsequent Theme: Verses 63–73
The following are the concluding verses of the Surah. They first contain a reminder of the Day of Judgment, stating that merely because the Prophet cannot tell its exact time or show it upon people's demand, taking it as a joke is an extremely short-sighted approach. It is an inevitable reality. Although the time of its occurrence is known only to Allah, it is bound to happen, and on that Day, no one will be able to act as a helper or intercessor for another; rather, every person will be held responsible for their own deeds and duties. On that Day, no one's excuse will be of any benefit—such as claiming that they merely followed their leaders and elders who led them astray.
After this, Muslims, and particularly the hypocrites, are warned not to adopt the same attitude of causing distress to their Messenger as the Jews did toward Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). Otherwise, their end will be the same as that of the Jews: Allah cleared Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) of every accusation and placed a perpetual curse upon his detractors. The correct path for Muslims, which guarantees success in both this world and the Hereafter, is to say 'We hear and we obey' to every command of the Messenger.
Finally, a warning is given that Allah has blessed humans with a great honor by making them trustees of His trust—a responsibility that no other creation in the heavens or the earth was deemed capable of bearing. The entire honor of a human depends on fulfilling the responsibilities of this trust. If a human fulfills its right, they are worthy of being prostrated to by angels; and if they fail to recognize this right, they deserve the lowest of the low (Asfal As-Safilin). The necessary consequence of this trust is that one day they will be held accountable for it, so that those who committed treachery are punished, and those who protected it receive their reward. Recite the verses in this light.
14. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses
'People ask you concerning the Hour. Say, "Knowledge of it is only with Allah." And what may make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near.' (63)
That is, when these evil-doers and mischief-makers are warned of the Day of Judgment, they make fun of it, saying that they have been hearing this threat for a long time, yet it has neither arrived nor will it ever arrive. If it has to come, why does it not come? When will the time of its arrival be? By this, they mean to imply that it is merely an empty threat with no reality, and they are not going to fall for it.
'Say, "Knowledge of it is only with Allah."' Tell them that as far as the time of its occurrence is concerned, its knowledge rests with Allah alone. Only God knows this secret of the universe. Neither do I know it, nor does anyone else have knowledge of it. However, its coming is certain, of which I am warning you. My inability to state its exact time does not negate its existence. In another place, this point is explained by stating that denying the existence of something simply because its time of occurrence is unknown is a foolish logic. Leaving the Day of Judgment aside, there are so many common realities in our daily lives whose exact time of occurrence is unknown, yet no sensible person denies them. Human knowledge is very limited. It cannot encompass everything. The testimonies of the Day of Judgment are present within the horizons, within ourselves, and within reason and nature. Therefore, making fun of it merely because its time cannot be specified is absolute foolishness and self-deception.
'And what may make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near.' This style of discourse highlights the magnitude, importance, and terror of the event. It is an answer to the sarcasm and mockery inherent in the opponents' question—implying: 'People are mocking you merely because you cannot state the exact time of the Day of Judgment, but what do you know, perhaps it has already drawn near...' This phrase also hints that there is not much delay left in its appearance. This is a source of consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and a very stern warning and admonition for those who mock the Day of Judgment. It should be kept in mind here that since the Prophet (pbuh) is the final Prophet, after his advent, the final stage remaining for the judgment of the world is the Day of Judgment. A detailed discussion on this will, God willing, follow in its proper place.

"'Indeed, Allah has cursed the disbelievers and prepared for them a Blaze. Abiding therein forever. They will not find any protector or helper.' (64–65)
This explicitly opens up the warning that was implied in the preceding verse: those who deny the Day of Judgment today should listen carefully that Allah has cursed them and prepared for them the punishment of Hell, wherein they will abide forever, and they will find no protector or helper therein. Neither will those partners and intercessors, upon whom they rely so complacently, be of any use to them, nor will this group and assembly of theirs, of which they are so proud in this world, be able to help them in the least."

"The Day their faces will be turned about in the Fire, they will say, 'How we wish we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Messenger.' And they will say, 'Our Lord, indeed we obeyed our masters and our dignitaries, and they led us astray from the [right] path. Our Lord, give them double punishment and curse them with a great curse.'" (66–68)
'Taqleeb' means turning over and over. Just as meat is roasted over a fire, sometimes from one side and sometimes from the other, it is stated that this is what their state will be. They will be dragged on their faces in the fire of Hell in such a way that both sides of their faces will be roasted. The face is mentioned specifically because it is the most prominent manifest location of the arrogance of turning away and showing pride against acknowledging the truth. When such is to be the fate of the face, other parts naturally follow. It is stated that on the Day they are mocking now, when the reality is unveiled to them in this manner, they will wish with deep regret: 'O, if only we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Messenger, and had not brought this ruin upon ourselves by falling for the deception of our leaders and elders!' On that Day, they will openly put the entire blame of their deviation on their leaders and elders, saying that they followed them and were led astray from the right path, and they will pray to God: 'O our Lord! Since they were the cause of our deviation, give them a double punishment compared to us, and curse them with a greater curse than the one that fell upon us because of them.' Here, only the request of these people is mentioned. The response that Allah will give to this request is mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 38: 'For each is double [punishment],' meaning both you and your leaders deserve a double punishment. The explanation of this has already been given in its proper place.
Here, two words 'saadatana' (our masters) and 'kubara'ana' (our dignitaries/elders) have been used. 'Saadah' obviously refers to political or social leaders and chiefs, while 'kubara' refers to ancestral and religious guides. Blind obedience to any of them is not permissible. Allah has endowed every human with intellect to distinguish between truth and falsehood. Therefore, it is the duty of every individual to utilize this touchstone. Whoever utilizes it, even if they stumble somewhere, it is hoped that Allah will support them and forgive them. But the one who blindly hands over their reins to others will meet the fate described in this verse.

"'O you who have believed, be not like those who abused Moses; then Allah cleared him of what they said. And he, in the sight of Allah, was distinguished.' (69)
The mention of the Day of Judgment was brought in the middle as a reminder and warning. Prior to that, the discussion was about those hypocrites who were active in causing mental and emotional distress to the Prophet (pbuh) and the believing men and women through their conspiracies and slanders. After this warning, the same theme is resumed, and Muslims are cautioned not to copy the style of the Jews who distressed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) at every step with their accusations, complaints, and covert conspiracies. Yet Allah cleared him of every accusation and slander, and lifted him from this world with great honor and respect; whereas those who tried to harm him were disgraced both in this world and the Hereafter. Although this address is general, it is specifically directed at the hypocrites.
Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to the children of Israel had caused distress, and his grievance is also mentioned in the Holy Qur'an through Hazrat Musa himself:
'And [recall] when Moses said to his people, "O my people, why do you harm me while you certainly know that I am the messenger of Allah to you?"' (As-Saff: 5)
In the Bible, Book of Deuteronomy Chapter 9, when Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) urged the children of Israel to advance upon Palestine and they showed their characteristic cowardice, Hazrat Musa rebuked them in these words:
'Remember, and do not forget, how you provoked the Lord your God to wrath in the wilderness. From the day you departed from the land of Egypt until you came to this place, you have been rebellious against the Lord.'
The Torah repeatedly mentions that whenever the children of Israel faced any trial, they blamed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and made him the target of their criticism. For instance, we refer to a few incidents here:
When the children of Israel faced trials after the message of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) in Egypt, they blamed him for all of it, saying that they had suffered calamities before his coming and continued to suffer after his coming. When Hazrat Musa led them out of Egypt and the Egyptians pursued them, the entire nation began to grumble: 'See, where has this man brought us to arrange our death?'
After crossing the sea, when Hazrat Musa went to Mount Sinai to receive the Torah, a large portion of the nation began to say: 'This man has left us here and we do not know where he has disappeared, and now he is not going to return.' Consequently, they had Samiri build a calf and began to worship it.
Qarun (Korah) formed a faction and spread the fitnah among the people that: 'This man (Hazrat Musa) alone has become the sole custodian of the entire religion and the leadership of the people, whereas in the eyes of the Lord, everyone is equal. This man has no superiority over us.'
During their life in the wilderness, when they faced difficulties regarding food and drink, they openly rebuked Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him): 'Was there no grave space for us in Egypt that you brought us here to die of hunger and thirst in the wilderness?'
In the wilderness, when Manna and Salva were provided for food and twelve springs were arranged for water, they started grumbling again: 'Our souls have dried up with this Manna and Salva; we remember the cucumbers, melons, garlic, and onions of Egypt.'
When Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) urged them to attack Palestine, they said: 'You want to make mincemeat of us with the swords of the mighty and tyrant inhabitants there. We are not ready for this. If there is to be a fight, you and your Lord go and fight. We are sitting right here.'
If one reads in the Torah the painful words in which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) repeatedly expressed his grief and sorrow over this attitude of the children of Israel, one can get some idea of the suffering he had to endure at the hands of his own nation. Pointing to this harassment by the children of Israel, Muslims are advised here: 'Do not deal with your Messenger in the manner that the children of Israel dealt with Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), otherwise you will meet the same end that they met.'
'Then Allah cleared him of what they said. And he, in the sight of Allah, was distinguished.' That is, Allah ultimately cleared Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) of every accusation. His truthfulness, good intention, and righteousness were revealed against every slander, and his enemies were disgraced and humiliated. He remained dignified, honored, and vindicated in the sight of Allah. His distinction shone in this world and will be bright in the Hereafter as well. This is an indirect glad tiding for the Holy Prophet (pbuh).

"'O you who have believed, fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice. He will [then] amend for you your deeds and forgive you your sins. And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly attained a great attainment.' (70–71)
After the instruction to avoid the way of the Jews, guidance is given to adopt the correct approach which is the demand of faith in Allah and His Messenger. It is said: 'fear Allah'—meaning, do not be among those who cause distress to Allah and His Messenger. He may delay in seizing the criminals, but when He does, no one can flee from His grasp, nor can anyone secure release from Him.
'And speak words of appropriate justice (qawlan sadida)'—that is, if you claim to believe, then say that which is the direct demand of this faith and its straightforward, essential requirement. This is an allusion toward acknowledging and declaring 'We hear and we obey.' This declaration validates the faith and opens up paths of guidance for the future. It also contains a subtle allusion to the statement of the Jews: 'We hear and we disobey.' The meaning is that the statement of the Jews was entirely twisted; they claimed to believe, yet their motto was as if 'We hear and we disobey.' If you wish to adopt the straight path of faith, then make your motto and declaration 'We hear and we obey.'
'He will amend for you your deeds and forgive you your sins.' This is described as the fruit of 'qawl sadid': if you eliminate the contradiction between your words and deeds, Allah will make your deeds fruitful, every crooked aspect of yours will become straight, and your every step will move in the correct direction. In this state, even if any mistake is made by you, Allah Almighty will overlook your faults. Allah does not abandon to the self and Satan those who wish to adopt the straight path.
'And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly attained a great attainment.'
