Based on the principle of gradual development of discourse, this phrase makes it clear that 'qawl sadid' refers indeed to the declaration of 'We hear and we obey.' It is stated that those who, after making the declaration of listening and obeying, obey Allah and His Messenger in every stage of life, achieve a very great success. The meaning is that this is not a losing bargain; rather, it is the key to eternal sovereignty, so let whoever wishes to win the game win it.

"'Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, and they declined to bear it and feared it; but man [undertook to] bear it. Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant. [It was] so that Allah may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the men and women who associate partners with Him and that Allah may accept repentance from the believing men and believing women. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful.' (72–73)
Now, the primary honor of humans is made clear: that they are trustees of Allah Almighty's covenant of obedience. This covenant is based on the freedom of choice and will, and for this reason, humans alone became deserving of it. This is because only someone possessing freedom of choice and will can be eligible for a covenant. No question of a covenant or pledge arises with creations that are compelled and powerless. Allah Almighty took this covenant from all the progeny of Adam, and this covenant is the basis of the vicegerency (Khilafah) granted to Adam and his progeny on this earth. To manifest the requirements of this vicegerency, Allah Almighty promised Adam (peace be upon him) to send His guidance, Book, and Shariah, and also warned him that those who follow My guidance will inherit Paradise, and those who violate it will be cast into Hell. To fulfill this promise, Allah Almighty sent His Prophets and Messengers, who renewed this covenant of obedience and servitude with their respective nations and warned the violators of its consequences.
Since this covenant is based on human free will, it acts as a double-edged sword. If humans, using their free will, fulfill the covenant of servitude to their Lord, no one is higher in the sight of Allah; and if they violate this covenant, it means they have turned the greatest honor gifted by God into the greatest disgrace for themselves.
Every covenant is a trust, and every trust necessarily demands that the one who deposited the trust will one day question how it was treated—whether its right was fulfilled or if there was treachery. This necessitates a Day of Judgment and Retribution. Therefore, Allah Almighty will gather everyone one day, place the records of their deeds before them, and decide who among the disbelievers and hypocrites deserve Hell, and who among the sincere believers deserve Paradise.
'Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, and they declined to bear it and feared it;'
From this verse, it is learned that Allah Almighty had offered this trust of voluntary obedience to the heavens, the earth, and the mountains, but they feared to bear this great responsibility and presented their excuse to be exempted from this heavy burden. This excuse of the heavens, the earth, and the mountains can be through the language of state (hal) as well as the language of speech (qal). Allah Almighty understands both the language of state and speech of His creation. It is clarified in the Qur'an that everything in the universe glorifies Allah Almighty, but only Allah understands their glorification; others do not understand it.
Similarly, a specific capability is required to bear any responsibility. If that capability is absent, bearing it is impossible. You cannot cultivate everything in every kind of soil. A small patch of land becomes the trustee of a single seed, not only protecting your trust but also nurturing and developing it. However, if you throw the same seed into a vast ocean, a massive mountain, or a barren desert, they cannot nurture it; rather, the seed will go to waste.
It is also a reality that if something lacks the capability to accept a certain thing, it will naturally decline it. For instance, our eye can bear only a certain degree of light; if the intensity of light exceeds that, the vision will be dazed. Similarly, our body can bear a certain degree of heat or cold; if the temperature exceeds that, our body will refuse to accept it and will fear it. Our stomach has the capability to accept only specific things. If we attempt to put anything else into it, regardless of how precious it might be, the stomach will not be able to bear it. This was the case with the heavens, the earth, and the mountains regarding this trust. They did not possess the capacity to bear it, and therefore they declined to undertake it.
'but man [undertook to] bear it.' This describes the honor of humans: that the burden of the trust which the heavens, the earth, the oceans, and the mountains could not bear was undertaken by humans. This reveals that although a human is a highly insignificant being in this universe in terms of physical existence, they are higher than the heavens, wider than the earth, and stronger and more elevated than the mountains in terms of spiritual and moral capabilities. Hence, this is why everything in this world was subjugated for them, but they were not subjugated to anything except the Lord of the worlds, before Whom bowing down was declared as the only source of dignity, and bowing to anyone else was declared a matter of disgrace.
'Indeed, he was unjust and ignorant.' This points to that very capability of humans by virtue of which they were deemed eligible for this trust. That is, this trust required that humans possess conflicting impulses so that they could be tested as to how they maintain the covenant of voluntary obedience to their Lord amidst the conflict of these opposing impulses, and how they discharge its responsibilities. Thus, they were made 'unjust' (zulum) and 'ignorant' (jahul). Injustice (zulm) is the opposite of justice (adl) and truth (haqq), and ignorance (jahl) is the opposite of knowledge (ilm) and forbearance (hilm). An 'unjust' person (zulum) is one who commits injustice despite possessing the consciousness of justice and truth. Similarly, an 'ignorant' person (jahul) is one who is overcome by ignorance and emotions despite possessing the capability of knowledge and tolerance. This very conflict is the test for humans, and it is the foundation of all their honor. If they remain firm on the path of justice despite possessing the freedom to choose the path of injustice solely for the sake of their Lord's pleasure, and control their base desires despite possessing the freedom to follow them solely out of fear of their Lord, then undoubtedly their status is higher than that of angels. This is because angels do not have to face any conflict in the path of serving God. Their path is entirely smooth, and their nature is completely unacquainted with the impulses of injustice and ignorance. But when a human worships, they do so by fighting their self and Satan at every step; hence, their servitude is higher than the servitude of angels. By the same token, just as humans are elevated the most due to this choice of theirs, they will also fall to the lowest depth if they fail to discharge the responsibilities of this choice. This same reality is clarified in Surah At-Tin:
'We have certainly created man in the best of statures; Then We return him to the lowest of the low, Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds.' (4–6)
'[It was] so that Allah may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the men and women who associate partners with Him and that Allah may accept repentance from the believing men and believing women. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful.' This states the consequence of this trust: its necessary outcome is that a Day must come when humans are questioned regarding this trust as to whether they fulfilled its right or not, so that those who adopted hypocrisy or committed shirk (associating partners) face the punishment for their treachery and breach of covenant, whether they be men or women; and those who fulfilled its right with firm faith become deserving of their Lord's mercy, whether they be men or women.
We have explained in another place that when 'Tawbah' (repentance) is followed by the preposition 'ala' (upon), it also encompasses the meaning of 'Rahm' (mercy). Therefore, this is a glad tiding for the believers: though this responsibility is very heavy, Allah Almighty is Forgiving and Merciful. He has kept the path of repentance open for His believing servants, men and women. If, due to some weakness, they commit any act of injustice or ignorance and then repent, Allah Almighty will accept their repentance and show mercy to them.
With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. This Surah is among the difficult Surahs of the Holy Qur'an, and I have hesitated to write on it. Although its difficulties, to the extent of my knowledge, were resolved by me a long time ago, the hesitation always remained as to whether the pen would be able to express what is in the heart and mind or not. Now, the discerning readers of this book will decide whether I have been able to overcome the difficulties and to what extent, but I have made a great effort. It was my desire to continue further reflection and contemplation on it, but the final time to deliver this trust had arrived, and therefore, whatever was in the mind has been put on paper. Accept what is correct, and overlook any slip or error. Human beings, after all, are unjust and ignorant. Allah Almighty is Forgiving and Merciful; to Him is the prayer to overlook the errors. Our final call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the Worlds.
Rahman Abad... 17 Shawwal, 1394 AH, 3 November, 1974 AD, Sunday 9:00 AM.
